The key difference is MiCBT's explicit integration with CBT techniques (exposure, assertiveness, Bipolar Exposure) and the use of validated assessment tools. Many people with existing meditation practices find that MiCBT deepens and contextualises their practice within a structured therapeutic framework.
faq.keywords: i practise other meditation traditions zen vipassana transcendental meditation is micbt compatible

faq.question: I'm worried that meditation will change me in ways I don't want.
faq.answer: MiCBT aims to develop specific, well-defined skills: sustained attention, equanimity, reduced automatic reactivity, and compassion. It does not aim to change your personality, values, or identity — it aims to reduce the suffering and unhelpful patterns that prevent you from living in line with your values. Many people describe feeling more like themselves after the program, rather than less. The changes that happen are largely in the direction of the qualities you already value: calm, clarity, kindness, and genuine engagement with life. These changes happen gradually and are under your awareness throughout.
faq.keywords: im worried that meditation will change me in ways i dont want

faq.question: I feel like I am a failure at meditation.
faq.answer: Feeling like a failure at meditation is almost universal at some point in practice — and it is itself a valuable object of observation. Notice: where do you feel this in your body? What is the quality of the sensation associated with 'failure'? Can you observe this without reacting to it? The feeling of failure is a thought — a self-evaluative story — and like all thoughts, it arises, exists for a moment, and passes. You cannot fail at meditation by practising. You can only practise well or less well, and both contribute to development. The very fact that you are noticing your experience critically means awareness is alive and working.
faq.keywords: i feel like i am a failure at meditation

faq.question: Can I use the MiCBT app in my research project?
faq.answer: Before using any MiCBT Institute resources or referencing MiCBT in a project, written approval from the MiCBT Institute is required. Please email admin@mindfulness.net.au with details of your project.
faq.keywords: can i use the micbt app in my research project

faq.question: I have an idea for improving the app.
faq.answer: We love receiving feedback! Please send your idea to support@mindfulness.net.au. Your feedback helps us understand what is working well and what could be improved, and supports the ongoing development of the program.
faq.keywords: i have an idea for improving the app

faq.question: How do I contact support?
faq.answer: For technical issues, account questions, or other enquiries: email support@mindfulness.net.au. Include a description of the issue and your device type (iPhone/Android) and operating system version if reporting a technical problem. For questions about the MiCBT Institute, training, or research: email admin@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: how do i contact support

faq.question: I can only see the depression graph in the DASS-21. How do I view the anxiety and stress graphs?
faq.answer: Tap the orange arrow above the graph. Each tap moves you to the next subscale graph. The DASS-21 tracks all three scales separately — Depression, Anxiety, and Stress — each with its own trend graph.
faq.keywords: i can only see the depression graph in the dass21 how do i view the anxiety and stress graphs

faq.question: How do I use the ES-16?
faq.answer: - Tap Assessments and select ES-16.- Read each statement and select a response indicating how much it applied to you over the past week.- Answer with your first instinct — don't spend too long on any item.- Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Complete it honestly — it is a tool for your own tracking and insight.
faq.keywords: how do i use the es16

faq.question: Should I focus on the Experiential Acceptance or Non-Reactivity subscale of the ES-16, or the total score?
faq.answer: Focus on the total ES-16 score. Research by Dr Alice Shires, Dr Bruno Cayoun, and the MiCBT research team shows that equanimity is a combination of both acceptance and non-reactivity, so the total score is the most accurate measure. The percentile score is also useful — it shows how your result compares with the general population. Individual subscale scores can give useful clues — for example, if non-reactivity is significantly lower than acceptance, it may indicate you are allowing sensations to be present but still acting on them — but treat these as supplementary rather than primary measures.
faq.keywords: should i focus on the experiential acceptance or nonreactivity subscale of the es16 or the total score

faq.question: How do I use the MSES-R?
faq.answer: - Tap Assessments and select MSES-R.- Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today.- Answer based on how confident you feel right now — not how you think you should feel.- Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Do not spend too much time on any statement.
faq.keywords: how do i use the msesr

faq.question: I can only see the Emotion Regulation subscale graph in the MSES-R. Are there graphs for the other subscales?
faq.answer: Yes — the MSES-R has six subscales, each with its own graph. To navigate between them, tap the orange right arrow above the graph. To go back, tap the left arrow. The six subscales are: Emotion Regulation, Equanimity, Social Skills, Distress Tolerance, Taking Responsibility, and Interpersonal Effectiveness.
faq.keywords: i can only see the emotion regulation subscale graph in the msesr are there graphs for the other subscales

faq.question: What are the six MSES-R subscales and what does each measure?
faq.answer: The MSES-R measures confidence in applying mindfulness skills across six domains: - Emotion Regulation — managing intense emotions without being overwhelmed.- Equanimity — observing experiences without reactivity.- Social Skills — engaging effectively and mindfully in social situations.- Distress Tolerance — tolerating difficult experiences without avoidance or reactive coping.- Taking Responsibility — recognising and owning the impact of your actions.- Interpersonal Effectiveness — communicating and relating to others in ways that reflect your values. The total score is the most direct measure of overall mindfulness-based self-efficacy.
faq.keywords: what are the six msesr subscales and what does each measure

faq.question: What are 'Community Norms' in the MSES-R?
faq.answer: Community Norms are the scores of the general population used as a benchmark to compare your own MSES-R results. They indicate how people without severe mental health conditions typically score on each subscale. The norms were derived from data collected from over 5,000 people internationally — primarily from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the USA, and the UK. Seeing your score relative to these norms gives a practical sense of how your confidence in applying mindfulness compares to a broad, healthy population.
faq.keywords: what are community norms in the msesr

faq.question: Which MSES-R result should I focus on most?
faq.answer: Focus on the total MSES-R score — it is the most direct measure of mindfulness-based self-efficacy and the primary metric to track across the program. The percentile score is also useful, as it shows how your result compares with the broader population. Individual subscale scores can reveal specific strengths and growth areas. For example, high Equanimity but lower Social Skills may indicate the formal meditation practice is progressing well while interpersonal application is still developing.
faq.keywords: which msesr result should i focus on most

faq.question: How do I use the SWLS?
faq.answer: - Tap Assessments and select SWLS.- Read each statement and select a response indicating how much you agree with it today.- Avoid comparing your life to others — base your answers on your own internal experience.- Tap Submit. There are no right or wrong answers. Answer honestly from your genuine felt sense rather than how you think you should feel.
faq.keywords: how do i use the swls

faq.question: How is my SWLS life satisfaction score calculated?
faq.answer: Your SWLS score is determined by comparing your answers to a large sample of people from diverse backgrounds. The scale produces a total score from 5 to 35, ranging from Extremely Dissatisfied to Extremely Satisfied relative to population norms. The app tracks your score over time so you can see how your sense of life satisfaction changes as you progress through the program.
faq.keywords: how is my swls life satisfaction score calculated

faq.question: My SWLS result doesn't seem accurate. Why?
faq.answer: The SWLS is very sensitive to how you answer. For an accurate result: - Answer each question honestly, without comparing your life to others — either those better off or worse off than you.- Base your answers on your own genuine experience of your life as it is, not how you think it should be.- Answer for how you genuinely feel today, not how you felt in the past or hope to feel in the future. Even small shifts in framing can significantly change your score, so consistency in how you approach the questions matters for meaningful trend tracking.
faq.keywords: my swls result doesnt seem accurate why

faq.question: How do I use the IAI?
faq.answer: - After completing a body scanning session, tap Assessments and select IAI.- Colour in all areas of the body shape where you felt any sensations during your practice — even very subtle sensations count.- To make colouring faster, increase the brush size by sliding the brush tool (left side of the screen) to the right.- When finished, tap Save. Be as accurate as possible. The app calculates the percentage of the body coloured and tracks this over time.
faq.keywords: how do i use the iai

faq.question: Can I undo a stroke in the IAI body map?
faq.answer: The current version of the app does not have an undo function for individual strokes. However, you can clear all colouring and start again by tapping Clear. Tip: adjust the brush size before you begin — larger brushes cover more area with each stroke, making colouring faster and reducing the need for corrections.
faq.keywords: can i undo a stroke in the iai body map

faq.question: Colouring the IAI body shape takes too long. Can I make the brush bigger?
faq.answer: Yes. Use the brush tool on the left-hand side of the body shape and slide the dot toward the right to increase the brush size. A larger brush covers more area per stroke, making colouring significantly faster. Tap Clear if you want to start over with a new brush size.
faq.keywords: colouring the iai body shape takes too long can i make the brush bigger

faq.question: Some of my past IAI dates have disappeared from the graph. Where did they go?
faq.answer: To keep the graph readable over time, the IAI graph displays only the first and last 6 measurements. All your results are still stored — scroll down below the graph to see a full list of every entry. You can delete any entry by swiping it to the left and tapping the trash icon. Note: if you delete an entry from the list, it will no longer appear on the graph either.
faq.keywords: some of my past iai dates have disappeared from the graph where did they go

faq.question: How do I use the MISS?
faq.answer: - After or during an intense experience, tap Assessments and select MISS.- Using the four sliding scales (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity), rate the qualities of the body sensations you felt.- Record your pre-equanimity rating — how the sensation was at its most intense.- Apply equanimity to those sensations for about 30 seconds.- Record your post-equanimity rating.- Make sure all four blue dots have been moved before tapping Save. The MISS will not save if any scale remains at its default position.
faq.keywords: how do i use the miss

faq.question: I can't see the Specificity indicators in the MISS. What are they?
faq.answer: The Specificity indicators appear when you tap or move the dot on a horizontal sliding scale — they do not display as static labels. The indicators for each scale are: - Mass: Loosest / Neutral / Densest- Temperature (Temp): Coldest / Neutral / Hottest- Motion: Still / Neutral / Most Movement- Fluidity: Lightest / Neutral / Heaviest Try tapping the scale line itself to make the dot and its indicator appear.
faq.keywords: i cant see the specificity indicators in the miss what are they

faq.question: How do I adjust the MISS dot positions after I've drawn the line?
faq.answer: Once the Fluidity dot is moved and the connecting line between all four dots is drawn, the individual values cannot be changed. To re-enter your values, tap the back arrow to return to the previous screen and re-enter all four ratings from scratch.
faq.keywords: how do i adjust the miss dot positions after ive drawn the line

faq.question: What do the MISS figures and graphs mean?
faq.answer: The MISS graph shows your pre- and post-equanimity ratings for each of the four sensation qualities (Mass, Temperature, Motion, Fluidity) across sessions. The most important result is the overall reduction in Intensity between your pre- and post-equanimity scores — a larger reduction means you are successfully using mindfulness to regulate the intensity of distressing body sensations. Don't feel overwhelmed by all the numbers — this overall Intensity change is your key metric.
faq.keywords: what do the miss figures and graphs mean

faq.question: How do I record my avoidance progress using the SUDS?
faq.answer: - Tap Assessments from the main menu and select SUDS.- Select the specific situation you want to review from your list.- Rate your current expected level of distress for that situation using the slider (0–100).- Tap Save. Repeat for each situation as often as you like — the more frequently you re-rate, the clearer your progress trend. The graph automatically tracks your average distress across all five situations over time.
faq.keywords: how do i record my avoidance progress using the suds

faq.question: What does the SUDS graph show?
faq.answer: The SUDS graph displays your average distress score across all five situations each time you review your progress. As you face these situations and your equanimity grows, the average should decrease over time. For example, if three situations are rated 60%, 65%, and 80%, the initial average shown on the graph would be approximately 68%. Each situation also has its own individual trend, which you can view by selecting it from the list.
faq.keywords: what does the suds graph show

faq.question: I'm not sure how to answer 'How long did you apply effort for?' in the ECD.
faq.answer: This question asks how long you managed to resist or reduce engagement in a potentially harmful behaviour — not how long the situation lasted. - Sometimes resistance is brief — a few minutes before giving in to an urge.- Sometimes it lasts much longer — hours of effort before eventually acting.- Sometimes you resist completely. There is no 'right' duration. The goal of the ECD is awareness and gradual progress. Even a few minutes of conscious effort represents genuine practice. Over time, you may notice that your effort duration increases as equanimity strengthens.
faq.keywords: im not sure how to answer how long did you apply effort for in the ecd

faq.question: Why do I need to track my ethics in the ECD?
faq.answer: Living more ethically reduces harm to yourself and others, and is a practical way of grounding compassion in daily life. However, despite good intentions, old habits and emotional reactions can make it genuinely difficult to act in alignment with your values. Without a tracking tool, it is easy to underestimate how often you encounter ethical challenges and overestimate how well you respond. The ECD makes these patterns visible so you can see your actual progress — not just your intentions.
faq.keywords: why do i need to track my ethics in the ecd

faq.question: When should I complete each assessment?
faq.answer: Each stage guide lists the suggested assessments for that stage. As a general guide: - DASS-21 — at the start of the program and at major milestones (Stages 1.1, 1.4, 2.2, 4.2).- ES-16 — regularly from Stage 1.3 onward.- MSES-R — from Stage 1.4 onward.- SWLS — at the start and end of the program, and at Stage 1.4.- IAI — after every body scanning session (or at least weekly) from Stage 1.3 onward.- MISS — after any intense emotional experience from Stage 1.3 onward.- SUDS — set up your five situations at Stage 2.1, then re-rate regularly throughout Stage 2.- ECD — daily from Stage 4.1 onward.
faq.keywords: when should i complete each assessment

faq.question: Does the app cost money? Is there a free trial?
faq.answer: Stage 1.1 of the MiCBT Guide app is completely free — you can complete the full first stage without any payment. After Stage 1.1, a subscription is required to continue to Stage 1.2 and beyond. For current pricing and subscription options, check the App Store (iPhone) or Google Play Store (Android).d) listing for the MiCBT Guide, or visit mindfulness.net.au. If you have a question about your subscription, billing, or a charge, contact support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: does the app cost money is there a free trial

faq.question: I was charged but can't access the app / my subscription isn't working.
faq.answer: - Make sure you are logged in to the correct account — the one used to purchase the subscription. Tap More > Settings to check.- Try tapping Restore Purchases in the app.- If the subscription is still not showing, contact support@mindfulness.net.au with your account email and a description of the issue. Include the order number or receipt from the App Store or Google Play if you have it.
faq.keywords: i was charged but cant access the app my subscription isnt working

faq.question: What is loving-kindness (metta) meditation and how do I practise it?
faq.answer: Loving-kindness meditation (metta in Pali) is the deliberate cultivation of goodwill — a sincere wish for wellbeing — toward yourself and others. In Stage 4.1, the practice begins with yourself: silently repeating phrases such as 'May I be peaceful. May I be free from suffering. May I be happy.' While repeating these phrases, bring attention to the body sensations that arise — warmth, openness, or perhaps resistance. Then extend the same wishes outward: - A loved one- A neutral person- A difficult person- All beings Loving-kindness is not about generating a particular emotion on demand. It is about setting a sincere intention and observing what arises with equanimity. If it feels forced or hollow, notice that reaction — it is valuable information about where resistance or self-judgment may be stored in the body.
faq.keywords: what is lovingkindness metta meditation and how do i practise it

faq.question: I notice involuntary movements or twitching during meditation — my fingers lift, my body sways. Is this normal?
faq.answer: Yes — involuntary movements during meditation are common and not dangerous. As interoceptive awareness deepens and the nervous system releases stored tension, many people notice subtle involuntary movements: fingers lifting, muscle twitches, slight swaying, or the body wanting to shift position. Observe these movements with equanimity, as you would any other sensation, without giving them undue significance or trying to suppress them. If the movements are large or distressing, briefly return attention to the breath and allow the body to settle.
faq.keywords: i notice involuntary movements or twitching during meditation my fingers lift my body sways is this normal

faq.question: I've been having unusual or vivid dreams since starting the program. Is this related to my practice?
faq.answer: Yes, very likely. The Co-Emergence Model helps explain it: as your sensitivity to body sensations increases through body scanning, the dream state — where sensations and emotions are processed — can become richer or more charged. Some people report more vivid, emotionally intense, or symbolic dreams during the early weeks of the program, particularly around Weeks 3–5 when body scanning is introduced and deepening. This is generally a sign that the practice is working — stored emotional content is being processed. If the dreams are disturbing or significantly affecting your sleep quality, mention this to a healthcare provider.
faq.keywords: ive been having unusual or vivid dreams since starting the program is this related to my practice

faq.question: What do my DASS-21 scores mean? How do I interpret them?
faq.answer: The DASS-21 produces separate scores for Depression, Anxiety, and Stress, each ranging from 0 to 42. Approximate severity ranges: - Normal: Depression 0–9 | Anxiety 0–7 | Stress 0–14- Mild: Depression 10–13 | Anxiety 8–9 | Stress 15–18- Moderate: Depression 14–20 | Anxiety 10–14 | Stress 19–25- Severe: Depression 21–27 | Anxiety 15–19 | Stress 26–33- Extremely Severe: Depression 28+ | Anxiety 20+ | Stress 34+ The most important use of the DASS-21 is tracking your own trend over time — a declining score across multiple measurements is the meaningful signal. If your scores are consistently in the moderate-to-severe range, please also seek support from a healthcare professional alongside using the app.
faq.keywords: what do my dass21 scores mean how do i interpret them

faq.question: Is the DASS-21 a clinical diagnosis?
faq.answer: No. The DASS-21 is a self-report screening tool, not a clinical diagnosis. It measures how you have been feeling over the past week and tracks changes over time. It cannot diagnose a mental health condition. If your scores are consistently in the moderate-to-severe range, or if you are concerned about your mental health, please consult a qualified healthcare professional.
faq.keywords: is the dass21 a clinical diagnosis

faq.question: Is there a book that goes with the app?
faq.answer: Yes. The app is the companion to Mindfulness-integrated CBT for Well-Being and Personal Growth (Second Edition, 2026), authored by Dr Bruno Cayoun and Dr Andrea Grabovac. The book provides a comprehensive written guide to the same 10-week program — including detailed explanations of the theory and practice, FAQs from past participants, and tables for tracking your progress. The app and book are designed to complement each other: the book offers depth and context, while the app delivers guided meditations, assessments, and practice tracking in a convenient format. The book is available from major booksellers.
faq.keywords: is there a book that goes with the app

faq.question: My equanimity score (ES-16) went down. Does this mean I'm getting worse?
faq.answer: Not necessarily. A lower ES-16 score at one point can reflect several things: - Increased self-awareness: As your practice deepens, you become more honest and precise in noticing how you actually react — which can temporarily lower your reported equanimity.- A challenging period: Significant life stressors can reduce equanimity temporarily without undoing your overall progress.- Natural fluctuation: Scores vary from week to week and a single data point is not meaningful on its own. What matters is the trend over the whole program. Compare your current score to your Stage 1.1 baseline — that is the meaningful comparison.
faq.keywords: my equanimity score es16 went down does this mean im getting worse

faq.question: I have an idea on how to improve the app
faq.answer: We love receiving feedback! If you have an idea you would like to share please send an email to support@mindfulness.net.au. We are always looking for ways to improve the app and make it a better experience for our users. Your feedback helps us understand what you like about the app and what could be improved. It also gives us insights into how we can better support you on your mindfulness journey. So please don't hesitate to reach out to us with any comments, ideas or concerns.
faq.keywords: i have an idea on how to improve the app

faq.question: How do I edit or delete a timer setting?
faq.answer: - Access your timers by going to More > My Timers. - To edit a timer, swipe right and tap the pen icon. - To delete, swipe left and tap the trash can icon.
faq.keywords: how do i edit or delete a timer setting

faq.question: How do I set a Reminder?
faq.answer: If you wish to receive regular reminders for your meditation practice, you can follow these easy steps: - Tap More and select My Reminders - Tap on the + icon to add a new reminder. - Choose the time of day when you want to receive the reminder. - Select the day(s) of the week when you want to receive the reminder. - Set the bell sound that you want to hear with the reminder. - Tap Save Reminder to finalise the setting.
faq.keywords: how do i set a reminder

faq.question: How do I edit or delete a Reminder setting?
faq.answer: - Access your Reminders by going to More > My Reminders. - To edit a Reminder, select it and make your changes. Tap Modify Reminder to save your changes. - To delete a Reminder, swipe left and select the trash can icon.
faq.keywords: how do i edit or delete a reminder setting

faq.question: Can I look ahead to see what's in the upcoming stages?
faq.answer: Yes, you can! Simply tap More, then toggle the switch 'on' next to Unlock All Stages. This will give you access to all steps and stages within the app.
faq.keywords: can i look ahead to see whats in the upcoming stages

faq.question: I can’t see the Specificity indicators mentioned in the MISS instructions.
faq.answer: The specifiers are as follows: - Mass: Loosest/Neutral/Densest - Temperature (Temp): Coldest/Neutral/Hottest - Motion: Still/Neutral/Most Movement - Fluidity: Lightest/Neutral/Heaviest The corresponding specifiers will appear by tapping or moving the dot on the horizontal line.
faq.keywords: i cant see the specificity indicators mentioned in the miss instructions

faq.question: Should I unlock all stages?
faq.answer: We recommend keeping the stages locked and working through them in sequence.MiCBT is a progressive program — each stage builds on the skills developed in the one before it. Jumping ahead before completing earlier stages can undermine the effectiveness of the training, because later practices assume a level of familiarity and stability developed in prior steps. Keeping stages locked also helps you stay focused on your current practice without being distracted or overwhelmed by content that isn't yet relevant to where you are in the program. If you are working with a therapist or trainer who has recommended that you skip ahead or access a specific stage, you can unlock stages from More > Settings > Stages.
faq.keywords: should i unlock all stages

faq.question: How can I avoid getting interrupted by notifications, text messages, and phone calls while I'm meditating?
faq.answer: You may hear notifications and receive phone calls during meditation. To avoid this, we recommend that you turn on the Do Not Disturb (DnD) Mode. iOS To turn on Do Not Disturb on your iPhone, you can follow these steps: - Swipe down from the top right of your display to open Control Center. - Long tap Focus. - Tap Do Not Disturb. Alternatively, you can also turn on Do Not Disturb by asking Siri to do it for you. Remember to disable this setting once your session is complete. When DnD is on, you will see a half-moon icon in the iPhone status bar ANDROIDTo set up Do Not Disturb on your Android device, follow these steps: - Open the Settings app - Scroll down and tap on Sound. - Tap on Do Not Disturb. - Tap on Turn On Now to activate Do Not Disturb mode. You can also customize your Do Not Disturb settings by selecting what to block or allow, setting up automatic rules, and more.
faq.keywords: how can i avoid getting interrupted by notifications text messages and phone calls while im meditating

faq.question: The slider doesn’t allow me to fast-forward or rewind the meditation instructions.
faq.answer: To move the audio forward or backward, you can tap the fast-forward or rewind button. Each tap will move the time by 5 seconds.
faq.keywords: the slider doesnt allow me to fastforward or rewind the meditation instructions

faq.question: Who is the Meditation Instructor?
faq.answer: Dr Bruno Cayoun is the meditation instructor. He is also the developer of the MiCBT program. We hope you don’t mind his French accent :)
faq.keywords: who is the meditation instructor

faq.question: When using the MISS, the orange slider doesn't move.
faq.answer: The sliding scales can be a little tricky sometimes! If you're having trouble, try changing the angle of your finger or using a different finger altogether. Don't give up!
faq.keywords: when using the miss the orange slider doesnt move

faq.question: How do I adjust the position of the dots in the MISS graph after the line has been drawn?
faq.answer: The values cannot be changed once the Fluidity dot is moved and the line between the dots is created. You can, however, tap the back arrow and re-enter the values.
faq.keywords: how do i adjust the position of the dots in the miss graph after the line has been drawn

faq.question: I can’t save the MISS
faq.answer: Make sure all the blue dots on the sliding scales have been moved. Then tap Save
faq.keywords: i cant save the miss

faq.question: What do 'Specificity' and 'Intensity' mean?
faq.answer: Specificity refers to the particular characteristics of a body sensation, commonly known as "the four elements" in ancient terminology. In the MISS, Mass represents the Earth element, Temp (temperature) represents the Fire element, Motion represents the Air element, and Fluidity (also known as solidity) represents the Water element. Therefore, the Specificity scales indicate which "elements" are predominantly activated in the body. On the other hand, Intensity measures how intense these specific sensations are. It also serves as a measure of distress, as more intense unpleasant sensations can be more distressing.
faq.keywords: what do specificity and intensity mean

faq.question: What do the figures and graphs mean?
faq.answer: The results of the MISS might be overwhelming, so you may want to focus only on the most important information. For instance, the app calculates the difference between pre and post-equanimity for a particular characteristic, such as Mass or Temperature, but you may only be interested in the overall result that reflects your ability to remain equanimous with an intense sensation for 30 seconds. The first figure shows the percentage of change in sensation specificity and intensity after your 30-second exposure to an intense body sensation with equanimity. Statistically speaking, this is called the "change score" using a "weighted distribution". This means that the amount of change at a higher initial level of distress will be calculated with a higher percentage of change than the same amount of change with a lower initial level of distress. This is because it takes more effort and equanimity to allow change with a higher level of distress. If you tap the right arrow above the Percentage of Change graph, you'll see the difference in Mass after your 30-second exposure to an intense body sensation with equanimity. This graph displays the "raw data". Tap through with the right arrow again, and you'll see the difference in Temperature, Motion and Fluidity after your 30-second exposure to an intense body sensation with equanimity. You can track your overall progress by tapping View Progress below any of the graphs. This will display the percentage of change for both the Specificity and the Intensity scales. All your other results for each measurement are also displayed below the graph. Please scroll down to see them all. If you want to remove any result set, swipe it to the left and tap the trash can icon. This will also remove it from the progress graph.
faq.keywords: what do the figures and graphs mean

faq.question: Can I “undo” the last stroke in the body shape?
faq.answer: This version of the app does not have an "undo" function. However, you can clear all of your colouring by tapping Clear.
faq.keywords: can i undo the last stroke in the body shape

faq.question: Some assessments dates have disappeared from the graph
faq.answer: To maintain a readable graph over time, only the first and last 6 measurements appear on the graph. However, you can scroll down through all your results listed below the graph. You can also delete any by swiping it to the left and tapping the trash can icon. If you delete a result from the list, it will no longer be visible on the graph.
faq.keywords: some assessments dates have disappeared from the graph

faq.question: What does Interoceptive Awareness mean?
faq.answer: Interoceptive awareness refers to the ability to feel sensations within the body, just as vision is the ability to see. It is the awareness of one's own body sensations. The IAI tool measures the percentage of interoceptive awareness over time and for each measurement. It is specifically designed to track the progressive interoceptive changes in the brain areas known as the "insular cortex" and "somatosensory cortex" resulting from meditation. It can also be used to measure changes in pain areas over time or at a given time.
faq.keywords: what does interoceptive awareness mean

faq.question: What is the Subjective Units of Distress Scale (SUDS)?
faq.answer: The Subjective Units of Distress Scale (SUDS) is a tool used to measure the level of distress one is likely to experience in a distressing situation. It involves rating your expected level of distress on a scale of 0 to 100, where 0 indicates no distress at all and 100 indicates the maximum distress imaginable. The SUDS tool calculates this rating in percentages. For instance, if you have a fear of heights, you may expect that looking down from the first floor of a building will cause you 40% distress, while looking down from the 10th floor of the same building will cause you 90% distress.
faq.keywords: what is the subjective units of distress scale suds

faq.question: How do I choose 5 situations to avoid if I have many?
faq.answer: If you're struggling to choose five situations, here's a helpful method. First, make a list of 10 to 12 situations you typically avoid. Next, choose five situations from this list that you think cause you an incremental level of distress. For instance, going out in the dark (20%), speaking with the neighbour about his tree (35%), socializing (60%), speaking in public (85%), and trying to start a relationship (100%). Keep in mind that while five situations are the minimum recommended for exposure in MiCBT, you can add more situations if you want.
faq.keywords: how do i choose 5 situations to avoid if i have many

faq.question: How do I record my progress with avoidance?
faq.answer: To keep track of your progress with avoidance, we use the SUDS tool. It is an easy and efficient way to monitor your progress. - To get started, tap on Assessments from the main menu - Choose Subjective Units of Distress Scale (SUDS) from the list. - Next, select the situation you want to review from the list below the graph. - Once you have selected the situation, rate your level of distress using the sliding scale. - Finally, tap on Update to add a review to this situation.
faq.keywords: how do i record my progress with avoidance

faq.question: How do I use the Interoceptive Awareness Indicator (IAI)?
faq.answer: The Interoceptive Awareness Indicator (IAI) is a tool that helps you assess your ability to feel body sensations during mindfulness practices like body scanning (Vipassana). - To use the IAI, colour in all the areas of the body shape where you feel any body sensations during body scanning. Be sure to colour in accurately and avoid leaving out any areas where you experience sensations. If you want to increase the brush size, you can use the brush icon on the left and drag the slider to the right to increase its size. - Once you have finished colouring in the front of the body shape, tap the right arrow at the top of the page to move on to the back. - When you're done, tap Done to see your results. The IAI calculates the percentage of interoceptive awareness for each measurement and over time.
faq.keywords: how do i use the interoceptive awareness indicator iai

faq.question: How do I use the Subjective Units of Distress (SUDS)?
faq.answer: The Subjective Units of Distress Scale (SUDS) is a self-assessment tool used to measure the level of distress experienced at any given moment. - Record a list of 5 situations you avoid because they create anxiety. Use 4-5 keywords for each situation, such as "driving into town". - Rate the level of distress that you expect each situation would cause you. You will need to enter and rate one situation at a time, then tap Save. Your situations will be listed in order of distress severity. The scale below can assist you in rating your distress level for each situation. 0: Serene and entirely undisturbed 10: Vigilant and concentrated without any sense of unease 20: Somewhat worried or perturbed, and a preference to avoid the situation 30: Anxious or troubled yet capable of preventing avoidance 40: Slightly to moderately anxious and preoccupied but still able to face the situation 50: Distressed, uneasy, and clear need to avoid the situation 60: Experiencing moderate to intense anxiety 70: Overwhelmed by anxiety 80: Anxious and panicky, losing concentration and feeling intense bodily sensations 90: Extremely anxious and desperate, feeling helpless and incapable of coping 100: Overwhelmingly distressed, rendering it nearly impossible to function and potentially on the brink of a breakdown.
faq.keywords: how do i use the subjective units of distress suds

faq.question: What is a “situation of potential harm”?
faq.answer: A situation of potential harm refers to any scenario in which your actions could potentially harm either yourself or someone else. This includes a range of behaviors such as taking someone's life, deceiving others, stealing, using harsh or divisive language, sexually exploiting someone, or consuming intoxicants. There are many other behaviors that can also be harmful, such as neglecting your child or driving recklessly, which can increase the risk of causing an accident or harming a person or animal.
faq.keywords: what is a situation of potential harm

faq.question: Why do I need to measure my ethics?
faq.answer: Living a more ethical life involves performing less harmful actions and practically grounding compassion in daily living. In compassion training, progress needs to benefit both the individual and others. However, despite having good intentions, old habits and emotions may arise, which can make it challenging to measure progress with compassion training objectively. Measuring progress with ethics is an effective way to measure progress with compassion training. It is worth noting that in Buddhist psychology and other wisdom traditions, compassion is a testimony of wisdom, as becoming wiser leads to becoming more compassionate.
faq.keywords: why do i need to measure my ethics

faq.question: What is the difference between acceptance and non-reactivity?
faq.answer: Acceptance and non-reactivity have similarities, but they are also different. For instance, when meditating, you may be able to resist reacting to an itch on your forehead or nose by not scratching it, but this doesn't necessarily mean that you have accepted it. On the other hand, you may accept that you feel pain in your back, but you might still move to improve your concentration.
faq.keywords: what is the difference between acceptance and nonreactivity

faq.question: Should I rely on the Experiential Acceptance or Non-reactivity Scale to understand my results?
faq.answer: The studies from Dr Alice Shires, Dr Bruno Cayoun, and others in the MiCBT research team suggest that equanimity is a combination of acceptance of our current experience and non-reactivity. Therefore, the most accurate measure of equanimity is the total score. However, it can also be helpful to look at the percentile score, as it allows you to compare your score with that of the general population in Australia. Generally, people without mental health conditions score around the 50th percentile, which corresponds to a total score of approximately 59 out of 80. The graph displays the percentile score for each measurement. High scores indicate high levels of self-reported equanimity.
faq.keywords: should i rely on the experiential acceptance or nonreactivity scale to understand my results

faq.question: What do the 6 subscales mean?
faq.answer: The MSES-R is a tool that measures the skills developed by individuals across six different life domains as a result of mindfulness practice. The tool is designed to assess one's confidence in achieving the original purpose of mindfulness, which is to reduce suffering. The MSES-R has six subscales to measure specific skills that people develop through mindfulness practice: The first subscale is Emotion Regulation, which measures an individual's ability to control their involuntary or subconscious emotional response. The second subscale is Equanimity, which measures the ability to accept challenging experiences without reacting. The third subscale is Social Skills, which measures an individual's abilities in the broad sphere of interactions with others. The fourth subscale is Distress Tolerance, which measures the ability to prevent avoidance and tolerate discomfort. The fifth subscale is Taking Responsibility, which measures the clarity of personal boundaries and the ability to take responsibility for one's experiences. The final subscale is Interpersonal Effectiveness, which measures the ability to connect intimately with others.
faq.keywords: what do the 6 subscales mean

faq.question: What do you mean by 'Community Norms'?
faq.answer: Community Norms refer to the scores of the general population that are used to compare your own scores. These scores indicate how individuals without severe mental conditions score on each of the subscales of the MSES-R. The MSES-R has derived these norms from data collected from over 5000 people internationally, primarily from Australia, Canada, New Zealand, the USA, and the UK. Your scores are compared to these norms.
faq.keywords: what do you mean by community norms

faq.question: Which result is the most important to consider?
faq.answer: The MSES-R tool provides separate measurements of each subscale, which can be helpful in understanding the specific skills you develop through your mindfulness practice. According to studies conducted by Dr Bruno Cayoun and the research team, the total score is the most direct measure of mindfulness-based self-efficacy. It is also useful to look at the percentile score, which enables you to compare your score with that of the general population. Most individuals without a mental health condition score around the 50th percentile, corresponding to a total score of 59/88. The graph displays the total score for each measurement. High scores indicate high levels of self-reported equanimity.
faq.keywords: which result is the most important to consider

faq.question: How is my level of satisfaction with life measured?
faq.answer: Your level of life satisfaction is determined by comparing your answers with a large sample of people from diverse backgrounds.
faq.keywords: how is my level of satisfaction with life measured

faq.question: The result I get is not accurate. Why?
faq.answer: The Satisfaction with Life Scale (SWLS) is very sensitive to how you answer the questions. To get an accurate rating, it is essential to answer the questions honestly without comparing your current life with those of others in your country or any other country. For example, you might be unhappy with your current situation, but if you compare it with people in a worse situation or an oppressed country, you might feel like you "shouldn't complain" or "should be happy" with what you have. However, if you answer the questions this way, your ratings will not reflect how you genuinely feel. Please answer the questions by thinking only about yourself.
faq.keywords: the result i get is not accurate why

faq.question: How do I get started with the app?
faq.answer: The MiCBT Guide is a 4-stage program teaching 10 sets of mindfulness-based skills. Here is how to begin. - Tap Home in the bottom navigation bar. - Tap Stage 1 (the circled number 1). - Tap 1 -- Committing to Self-Care, then tap Start. - Set your goals -- describe what you would like to work on and rate how distressing it currently feels. - Watch Dr Cayoun's introduction video to understand the stage. - Read the Readings before moving on to the meditations. - Work through the guided meditations in order. After each session, tap Mark as Complete on the Timer Settings screen. - Complete the suggested Assessments at the bottom of the stage page. - Log your practice in Diary after each session. - Set reminders via More → My Reminders to keep your practice consistent. How long does each stage take? Aim for roughly one stage per week, but take longer if you need to -- there is no benefit to rushing. Complete each stage before moving on to the next. What does each stage page show? Each stage page includes an Instructions card, an Introduction video by Dr Bruno Cayoun, and a Readings section. Always complete the readings before beginning the meditations.
faq.keywords: how do i get started with the app

faq.question: How do I delete all my data?
faq.answer: To delete your account: open the app, tap More (bottom nav) > My Profile > Delete Account. This permanently removes your account and all associated data. If you only want to remove local app data without deleting your account: iPhone: Settings > General > iPhone Storage > MiCBT Guide > Delete App. Android: Settings > Apps > MiCBT Guide > Storage > Clear Data.
faq.keywords: how do i delete all my data

faq.question: How do I share records with my therapist?
faq.answer: You can share your progress with your therapist using the built-in share button available in each assessment and exercise. Tap the share icon to export that specific result as an image or PDF and send it via email or messaging. For a broader overview, you can also take screenshots of your assessment graphs and diary entries and send them via your preferred platform. If you have a suggestion for improving how records are shared, email support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: how do i share records with my therapist

faq.question: Audio isn't playing or keeps stopping
faq.answer: If you are having trouble with audio in the MiCBT Guide, work through these steps in order: - Check your volume -- make sure your device volume is turned up and not set to silent. - Check Do Not Disturb -- if Do Not Disturb is enabled, it may be silencing the app. See our guide on turning off Do Not Disturb for iOS and Android. - Check your internet connection -- audio files are streamed, so a weak connection can cause interruptions. Try switching to a stronger Wi-Fi or mobile data connection. - Close and reopen the app -- force-close the app completely and reopen it, then try playing audio again. - Restart your device -- restarting your phone resolves many audio-related issues. If none of these steps help, please contact us at support@mindfulness.net.au and include your device model and operating system version.
faq.keywords: audio isnt playing or keeps stopping

faq.question: The app keeps crashing or won't load
faq.answer: If the MiCBT Guide is crashing or not loading correctly, try these steps: - Close and reopen the app -- force-close completely and reopen. - Check for updates -- make sure you have the latest version installed. Go to the App Store (iPhone) or Google Play Store (Android) and check for updates. - Restart your device -- a simple restart resolves many app issues. - Reinstall the app -- if the problem continues, delete the app and reinstall it from the App Store or Google Play. Note: reinstalling will remove locally stored data, but your account and server-stored data will remain intact. If the problem persists after reinstalling, please contact us at support@mindfulness.net.au with your device model, operating system version, and a description of what happens when the app crashes.
faq.keywords: the app keeps crashing or wont load

faq.question: My data isn't saving
faq.answer: If your practice logs, assessment results, or goals do not appear to be saving, try the following: - Check your internet connection -- the app requires an active connection to save data to your account. Make sure you are connected to Wi-Fi or mobile data. - Check you are logged in -- tap More to confirm you are signed in to your account. - Try again -- complete the action again (log a session, submit an assessment, etc.) and make sure you tap Submit or Save before closing the screen. If data is consistently not saving, please contact us at support@mindfulness.net.au.
faq.keywords: my data isnt saving

faq.question: How do I edit or update my goals?
faq.answer: To edit or update a goal: - Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar. - Tap the pen icon next to the goal you would like to update. - Tick any progress indicators you have achieved. - Tap Update to save your changes. It is normal for your goals to evolve as you progress through the program. We recommend reviewing them at the end of each stage to reflect how things have changed.
faq.keywords: how do i edit or update my goals

faq.question: How do I mark a goal as complete?
faq.answer: When you have achieved a goal: - Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar. - Tap the pen icon next to the goal. - Tap Complete. - Confirm by tapping Goal Achieved? The goal will be marked as completed and removed from your active goals list.
faq.keywords: how do i mark a goal as complete

faq.question: How do I delete a goal?
faq.answer: To delete a goal: - Tap Goals in the bottom navigation bar. - Swipe left on the goal you would like to remove. - Tap the trash icon. Note: deleting a goal is permanent and cannot be undone. If you have achieved a goal rather than wanting to remove it, consider marking it as complete instead -- that preserves your record of progress.
faq.keywords: how do i delete a goal

faq.question: Stage 1.1 - Establishing Self-Care and Commitment
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Mindfulness of breath -- 20 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.1 is about establishing the foundations: a regular practice schedule, a clear commitment to yourself, and your first experiences of formal mindfulness meditation. The practice is breath awareness. Sit comfortably, close your eyes, and bring your attention to the natural movement of the breath -- usually at the nostrils or the rising and falling of the abdomen. When your mind wanders (and it will -- this is completely normal), gently return attention to the breath. What you are building is the ability to sustain attention. The wandering mind is not a failure -- noticing that you have wandered and returning is the practice itself. Each return is a repetition of the skill. This stage also asks you to set goals. These are your anchors -- specific, honest descriptions of what you would like to change or improve. They will be your reference point throughout the program. Suggested assessments this stage: DASS-21, SWLS, MSES-R, ES-16.
faq.keywords: stage 11 establishing selfcare and commitment

faq.question: Stage 1.2 - Regulating Attention
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Mindfulness of breath -- 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.2 extends breath awareness to 30 minutes and deepens the work of attention regulation. With more time in practice, you will likely notice more of what the mind does -- the stories it tells, the places it wanders to, the pull of particular thoughts or feelings. The instruction remains the same: notice where attention goes, and return it to the breath. At this stage you may begin to notice something else -- body sensations that arise alongside thoughts. This is the beginning of interoceptive awareness. Do not try to analyse the sensations yet. Just notice, and return to the breath. The deeper engagement with sensations comes in the next stage. Suggested assessments this stage: IAI.
faq.keywords: stage 12 regulating attention

faq.question: Stage 1.3 - Understanding and Regulating Emotions
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Body scanning -- 30 minutes, twice daily. Stage 1.3 introduces the body scan -- the central practice of MiCBT. Instead of resting attention on the breath, you now move it systematically through the body, part by part, noticing whatever sensations are present. This is where the connection between emotions and body sensations becomes real rather than theoretical. When you scan an area that holds tension, discomfort, or subtle unease, you are encountering the physical layer of emotional experience. The instruction is equanimity: observe what is there without trying to change it, make it stay, or make it go. This is easier said than done -- the natural tendency is to react. Noticing that tendency, without acting on it, is the skill you are developing. The MISS (Mindfulness of Sensations Scale) is introduced this stage. Use it when strong sensations arise during practice -- it helps you measure and develop equanimity in real time. Suggested assessments this stage: IAI, DASS-21.
faq.keywords: stage 13 understanding and regulating emotions

faq.question: Stage 1.4 - Applying Mindfulness in Daily Life
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Body scanning + mindful activities. Stage 1.4 begins the transfer of mindfulness skills into daily life. Alongside your formal body scanning sessions, you start to bring mindful awareness into everyday activities -- eating, walking, washing dishes, conversations. This is not about doing things slowly or reverently. It is about noticing what is actually happening in each moment -- what you are sensing, what is arising in the body -- rather than moving through life on autopilot. Review your goals this stage. You have now had several weeks of practice. Has anything shifted? Do your goals still accurately reflect what you want to work on? Suggested assessments this stage: DASS-21, SWLS, MISS, MSES-R, ES-16.
faq.keywords: stage 14 applying mindfulness in daily life

faq.question: Stage 2.1 - Regulating Behaviour and Overcoming Avoidance
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Bilateral body scanning -- SUDS hierarchy work. Stage 2 introduces bilateral scanning -- a more intensive practice where attention moves across the body from side to side. This is combined with exposure work: systematically facing situations and experiences you have been avoiding. The SUDS (Subjective Units of Distress Scale) is your tool this stage. You identify up to five situations you have been avoiding and rate how distressing each feels. Starting with the least distressing, you begin to approach these situations while maintaining equanimity -- using your body scanning skills to stay present rather than react. This stage often brings up resistance. That is expected. Avoidance is maintained precisely because approaching feels uncomfortable. Your equanimity practice is what makes it possible to do this work without being overwhelmed. Suggested assessments this stage: SUDS, MISS, DASS-21.
faq.keywords: stage 21 regulating behaviour and overcoming avoidance

faq.question: Stage 2.2 - Improving Self-Confidence
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Bilateral body scanning -- Bipolar Exposure. Stage 2.2 continues the exposure work of Stage 2.1, with a focus on self-confidence. Bipolar Exposure means working with both ends of a response -- not only reducing distress around feared situations, but also developing equanimity toward the positive experiences that follow successful engagement. Review your SUDS ratings. You should be seeing movement -- situations that once felt highly distressing becoming more manageable. This is not about forcing yourself to feel fine. It is about noticing that with equanimity, the distress changes on its own. Suggested assessments this stage: SUDS, MISS, DASS-21, MSES-R.
faq.keywords: stage 22 improving selfconfidence

faq.question: Stage 3.1 - Interpersonal Mindfulness
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Bilateral scanning -- Interpersonal awareness. Stage 3 extends mindfulness into relationships. Stage 3.1 focuses on interpersonal awareness -- noticing how you respond in social situations, what arises in the body when you are with others, and how habitual patterns play out in interaction. The MSES-R (Mindfulness-based Self-Efficacy Scale -- Revised) is particularly relevant here. Pay attention to the Social Skills and Interpersonal Effectiveness subscales as a measure of progress. Interpersonal mindfulness does not mean becoming passive or accommodating in relationships. It means responding from awareness rather than reactivity -- a significant shift for most people, and one that tends to change relationships in meaningful ways. Suggested assessments this stage: MSES-R, MISS, DASS-21.
faq.keywords: stage 31 interpersonal mindfulness

faq.question: Stage 3.2 - Mindful Communication
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Bilateral scanning -- Mindful speech practice. Stage 3.2 focuses specifically on communication -- how you speak, listen, and engage. Mindful speech practice involves noticing body sensations before and during communication: what arises when you want to say something difficult, when you feel unheard, or when conflict is present. The ES-16 (Equanimity Scale) is particularly relevant this stage. It measures both Experiential Acceptance and Non-Reactivity -- two dimensions of equanimity that matter enormously in interpersonal contexts. Review your goals. Many goals set at the beginning of the program have an interpersonal dimension. How have they shifted? Suggested assessments this stage: ES-16, MSES-R, MISS, DASS-21.
faq.keywords: stage 32 mindful communication

faq.question: Stage 4.1 - Cultivating Compassion
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Loving-kindness meditation -- Bilateral scanning. Stage 4 introduces loving-kindness (metta) meditation -- a practice of deliberately cultivating goodwill toward yourself and others. After three stages of inward work, this stage turns attention outward. Loving-kindness can feel forced at first, especially if self-compassion is difficult. That reaction is worth noticing -- it is information. The practice is not about manufacturing feelings; it is about setting an intention and noticing what arises. The ECD (Ethical Challenges Diary) is also introduced this stage. It is a reflection tool for noticing situations where you have the opportunity to act in alignment with your values -- and tracking how you respond. Suggested assessments this stage: ECD, MSES-R, DASS-21, ES-16.
faq.keywords: stage 41 cultivating compassion

faq.question: Stage 4.2 - Maintaining Wellbeing and Growth
faq.answer: Practice this stage: Full practice integration -- Relapse prevention. Stage 4.2 is the culmination of the program. All the practices come together: breath awareness, body scanning, bilateral scanning, loving-kindness, the assessments, the ECD. The focus this stage is on sustainability. What does your practice look like when the structure of the program ends? What habits have genuinely changed? What do you want to carry forward? Review all your goals. Compare your current assessment scores with your Stage 1.1 baselines. This is a moment to acknowledge what has shifted -- not just in scores, but in how you meet your experience day to day. The end of the program is not the end of practice. It is the beginning of practising on your own terms, with all the skills you have developed. Suggested assessments this stage: ECD, DASS-21, SWLS, MSES-R, ES-16, SUDS.
faq.keywords: stage 42 maintaining wellbeing and growth

faq.question: My mind keeps wandering -- what should I do?
faq.answer: A wandering mind is one of the most common experiences in meditation -- and it is not a problem. It is the practice. The instruction in mindfulness meditation is not to keep a blank mind. The instruction is to notice when attention has wandered and return it to the object of meditation (the breath, or body scanning). That noticing and returning is the practice itself. Each time you do it, you are training attention. A few things that help: practice at the same time each day so the routine becomes familiar; sit upright rather than lying down; if a particular thought keeps recurring, acknowledge it briefly -- "planning" or "worrying" -- and return; try not to judge the wandering. It says nothing about how good your practice is. If your mind is especially busy, it is sometimes a sign that you need this practice more than usual. Try bringing the same equanimity to the busy mind that you bring to body sensations.
faq.keywords: my mind keeps wandering what should i do

faq.question: What is Mindfulness-integrated Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (MiCBT)?
faq.answer: MiCBT combines mindfulness practices with traditional Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) techniques. It aims to enhance emotional regulation, reduce stress, and improve mental clarity by encouraging present-moment awareness and thoughtful responses to thoughts and feelings.
faq.keywords: what is mindfulnessintegrated cognitive behavioral therapy micbt

faq.question: What are the benefits of MiCBT?
faq.answer: The benefits of MiCBT include enhanced emotional regulation, stress reduction, improved mental clarity, greater self-awareness, and long-term mental well-being. It helps individuals manage their thoughts and emotions more effectively and promotes a balanced and fulfilling life.
faq.keywords: what are the benefits of micbt

faq.question: How does MiCBT differ from traditional CBT?
faq.answer: While traditional CBT focuses on changing negative thought patterns and behaviors, MiCBT integrates mindfulness practices to increase present-moment awareness and acceptance. This combination allows for a more holistic approach to mental wellness, addressing both cognitive and emotional aspects.
faq.keywords: how does micbt differ from traditional cbt

faq.question: How does the MiCBT Guide app support users in practicing MiCBT?
faq.answer: The app provides step-by-step video guidance from Dr. Bruno Cayoun, interactive exercises to reinforce MiCBT practices, personalised reminders to maintain consistency, progress tracking to monitor improvements, and goal-setting features to achieve objectives.
faq.keywords: how does the micbt guide app support users in practicing micbt

faq.question: What are the key features of the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: Key features include: - Expert Video Support: Step-by-step guidance from Dr. Bruno Cayoun. - Interactive Exercises: Activities designed to develop coping skills and resilience. - Personalised Reminders: Custom notifications to stay consistent with your practice. - Progress Tracking: Access to industry-standard assessments to monitor your journey. - Goal Setting: Tools to set and achieve goals. - Weekly Readings and Guided Meditations: Fresh content to keep your practice dynamic.
faq.keywords: what are the key features of the micbt guide app

faq.question: How can I track my progress in the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: The app features an intuitive progress tracking dashboard that allows you to monitor your improvements over time. This feature helps you stay motivated and committed to your MiCBT practice by visualizing your achievements.
faq.keywords: how can i track my progress in the micbt guide app

faq.question: Can I set personalised reminders in the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: Yes, the app allows you to set personalised reminders to ensure you never miss a meditation session. You can tailor notifications to fit your schedule and maintain a consistent practice.
faq.keywords: can i set personalised reminders in the micbt guide app

faq.question: How do the interactive exercises in the MiCBT Guide app work?
faq.answer: The interactive exercises are designed to develop adaptive coping skills and resilience. These activities are tailored to reinforce MiCBT principles, making your practice engaging and effective.
faq.keywords: how do the interactive exercises in the micbt guide app work

faq.question: Is there expert support available in the app?
faq.answer: Yes, the app includes expert video support from Dr. Bruno Cayoun, providing practical advice and detailed explanations to help you master MiCBT techniques.
faq.keywords: is there expert support available in the app

faq.question: What type of content can I expect in the weekly readings and guided meditations?
faq.answer: The weekly readings and guided meditations offer fresh perspectives and techniques to enrich your MiCBT practice. This content is designed to keep your mindfulness journey dynamic and inspiring.
faq.keywords: what type of content can i expect in the weekly readings and guided meditations

faq.question: How do I get started with the MiCBT Guide app?
faq.answer: Download the MiCBT Guide app from the App Store (iPhone) or Google Play Store (Android). Create an account using your email address and password, or continue with Google, Facebook, or Apple. Stage 1.1 is free to complete. After Stage 1.1, a subscription is required to continue. To begin: tap Stage 1 on the Home screen, then tap Step 1.1 (Committing to Self-Care). Tap Start, set your goals, watch Dr Cayoun's introduction video, then work through the Readings and Meditations in order. Log your practice after each session using the Log My Practice button. Set reminders via More > My Reminders.
faq.keywords: how do i get started with the micbt guide app

faq.question: I'm feeling overwhelmed during body scanning
faq.answer: Strong sensations and emotions sometimes arise during body scanning. This is normal -- not a sign that something has gone wrong. When you bring sustained, equanimous attention to an area of the body, sensations that were there all along become more noticeable. What feels overwhelming may simply be a level of awareness you were not accessing before. The instruction remains the same: observe without reacting. If a sensation is very intense, try narrowing your attention -- scan a smaller area, or return to the breath for a few minutes. Use the MISS to measure and develop equanimity with the sensation rather than trying to push it away. If you are experiencing emotional distress that feels significant -- particularly if it is linked to trauma -- please contact your therapist. Body scanning can sometimes surface material that is best explored with professional support alongside the program.
faq.keywords: im feeling overwhelmed during body scanning

faq.question: I'm feeling nothing during body scanning
faq.answer: Feeling very little during body scanning is normal -- especially in the early stages of practice. Interoceptive awareness (sensitivity to internal body sensations) develops over time. If you are feeling very little, you are not doing it wrong. You are at an early point in developing this awareness, and it will deepen with consistent practice. A few things to try: slow down and spend more time in each body part; bring a gentle curiosity rather than waiting for something dramatic; notice even the subtlest sensations -- temperature, pressure, the contact of clothing against skin. These small sensations are real and worth noticing. The IAI (Interoceptive Awareness Indicator) can be a useful reflection tool at this stage. Even if what you notice feels minor, try to map it -- the act of articulating sensation helps develop the sensitivity you are building.
faq.keywords: im feeling nothing during body scanning

faq.question: I feel sleepy or keep falling asleep during meditation
faq.answer: Torpor -- sleepiness during meditation -- is one of the classic challenges of practice, especially in the early weeks or during evening sessions. A few things that help: sit upright with your spine self-supporting rather than leaning against a wall or chair back; keep your eyes slightly open, directed downward at a soft angle; meditate at a time when you are naturally more alert; if you feel drowsiness coming on, take a few deeper breaths, then return to regular practice. Sleepiness sometimes signals genuine fatigue -- make sure you are getting adequate sleep. Meditation is not a substitute for rest, and a significantly sleep-deprived person will struggle to sustain attention regardless of practice quality. It can also signal avoidance: the mind drifting away from whatever is arising in practice. If this pattern is consistent, it is worth bringing it up with your therapist.
faq.keywords: i feel sleepy or keep falling asleep during meditation

faq.question: I want to stop -- I'm not sure the program is working
faq.answer: This feeling is very common, and it almost always passes. The question worth sitting with is: what is it that wants to stop? Sometimes it is a phase in practice where things feel harder before they feel easier. Somewhere between Stage 1 and 2, many people hit a wall -- the novelty has worn off, the skills have not fully settled yet, and the effort feels high relative to the benefit. This is temporary. Sometimes the discomfort is meaningful -- the practice is surfacing something real. That is not a reason to stop; it may be the most important time to continue, ideally with support from your therapist. Sometimes there are practical obstacles: practice time is hard to maintain, life got busy, you fell out of the habit. If this is the case, return without judgment. Start with one session rather than trying to catch up all at once. If you have been working consistently and genuinely feel the program is not right for you, please discuss it with your therapist. They can help you evaluate whether to continue, adjust the pace, or take a planned pause.
faq.keywords: i want to stop im not sure the program is working

faq.question: My subscription isn't showing on my new device
faq.answer: If you have switched to a new phone and your MiCBT Guide subscription is not showing as active, your subscription is still valid -- it just needs to be restored on your new device. Step 1 -- Make sure you are logged in to the same account Your subscription is linked to your account, not your device. Open the app and confirm you are signed in with the same email address you used when you purchased the subscription. If you signed up using Google, Facebook, or Apple, tap the same option you used originally on the login screen. Step 2 -- Restore your purchase You can restore through your device's app store: - iPhone / iPad: Open the App Store, tap your profile icon, then tap Subscriptions to confirm your subscription is active. - Android: Open the Google Play Store, tap your profile icon, then go to Payments & subscriptions → Subscriptions to confirm. Still not working? Contact us at support@mindfulness.net.au with: - The email address associated with your account - The type of subscription you purchased (monthly or annual) - Whether you are on iPhone or Android We will look into it and get back to you promptly.
faq.keywords: my subscription isnt showing on my new device

section.name: Book Q&As — MiCBT Guide 2nd Edition (Bruno A. Cayoun & Andrea D. Grabovac)

faq.question: I'm not able to relax despite really trying to. I can only get about 20% of my body to relax. It s frustrating, and I'm starting to dislike this exercise. Am I doing it wrong?
faq.answer: It might seem counterintuitive, but the purpose of PMR isn't to achieve 100% relaxation. The real goal is to carefully notice the contrast between tension (when you tighten the muscles) and relaxation (when you release that tension). This week, you're training your mind to recognize these differences, which will help you notice and release tension more effectively in daily life. WEEK 1: ESTABLISHING SELF-CARE AND COMMITTING TO CHANGE 41 Interestingly, the sessions where relaxation feels difficult are often the most valuable. So, when you notice that it's hard to relax muscles after tight ening them, approach this as a learning experience! Being without aversion toward tension and without craving for relaxation will help you be more accepting of the unavoidable tensions that arise in daily life, even those that you cannot readily release.
faq.keywords: im not able to relax despite really trying to i can only get about 20 of my body to relax it s frustrating and im starti

faq.question: I have bulging discs in my back, and I feel scared of tensing my back in case I injure it further.
faq.answer: It s important to be gentle with any areas that require special care. When the instructions suggest arching your back, adapt the movement to suit your body s needs. For example, you can substitute a gentle stretch or, if necessary, simply bring your awareness to the area and notice whatever sen sations are already present, without adding any small movements. In general, even when the audio instructions are to tighten muscles even tighter, avoid overly tensing muscles. The appropriate amount is one that doesn't hurt.
faq.keywords: i have bulging discs in my back and i feel scared of tensing my back in case i injure it further

faq.question: I feel anxious when I do PMR. Am I doing something wrong, or is this just too difficult for me?
faq.answer: It s common to feel unsettled during the first few PMR sessions. However, the practice of PMR does not create anxiety in and of itself. This anxiety can stem from worries about not performing the exercise correctly, from taking time away from other activities or responsibilities, or from bringing physical sensations of anxiety that were already there into greater awareness. As PMR itself does not train skills to address anxious thoughts, relaxing during practice can allow existing concerns and anxieties to become more prominent. During your practice, do your best to disengage from any anxious or dis tracting thoughts that may be pulling your attention away from the body. Then refocus attention on the physical sensations of tensing and relaxing each muscle group. If anxiety persists, try practicing with your eyes open for the first few sessions. Once you feel more comfortable, gradually shift to practicing with your eyes closed.
faq.keywords: i feel anxious when i do pmr am i doing something wrong or is this just too difficult for me

faq.question: I have some flashbacks during PMR. I can handle them, but I wonder if I would be better off skipping this practice.
faq.answer: Flashbacks during PMR are quite normal and nothing to worry about in themselves. Relaxation lowers mental activity and allows stored memories — including those linked to past difficult experiences — to surface naturally. This is the same mechanism that produces intrusive thoughts during meditation generally. The practice is to stay in the present moment and observe the body sensations that co-emerge with the memory, rather than following or nourishing the memory itself. If you can observe with some equanimity, continue. Keep your eyes open if that helps you stay grounded. However, if flashbacks are causing significant distress or you find you cannot stop ruminating after the session, it is worth discussing this with a therapist. They can help you work with traumatic material in a supported way while continuing the program.
faq.keywords: i have some flashbacks during pmr i can handle them but i wonder if i would be better off skipping this practice

faq.question: I've practiced other forms of relaxation before, and it was suggested that I lie down. Can I lie down with this one, too?
faq.answer: It s better to practice PMR while seated for two main reasons. First, lying down can lead to drowsiness, and one of the key goals of PMR is to learn how to remain fully awake while feeling deeply relaxed so you can apply this skill in daily life. All upcoming practices, such as mindful ness of breath and body-scanning, will also be practiced while seated, as this helps maintain alertness. Training yourself to be alert and deeply relaxed while seated during PMR this week will set you up for more alert, effective meditation practices next week! Posture plays an important role in supporting this alert, wakeful mental state.
faq.keywords: ive practiced other forms of relaxation before and it was suggested that i lie down can i lie down with this one too

faq.question: I'm struggling to make time for practice. Can I just practice once instead of twice daily?
faq.answer: While practicing once daily is better than not practicing at all, research shows that more frequent practice leads to better results [16]. You ll likely experience greater benefits if you commit to practicing twice daily. If finding time feels challenging, consider reor ganizing your activities so your self-care doesn't come last again. We often prioritize others needs out of duty or compassion, but neglect our own needs. This program is an opportunity to improve self-care, which will also support the development of self-compassion in Stage 4 of the program. Our brains have evolved to prioritize tasks based on what we expect the rewards to be. If you currently find it more rewarding to focus on others needs than your own, it's natural to prioritize their care over your own. Behavioral psychology explains this as a conditioned response, where per ceived gains or losses shape our actions. These conditioned tendencies can make it hard to prioritize self-care, including practicing twice daily. Changing this pattern involves recognizing that investing in your well-being benefits not only you but also those around you. WEEK 1: ESTABLISHING SELF-CARE AND COMMITTING TO CHANGE 43 Instead of overthinking, try to approach twice-daily practice as a nonnegotia ble part of your day. Developing the habit of consistent self-care is one of most valuable outcomes arguably even more important than learning to relax!
faq.keywords: im struggling to make time for practice can i just practice once instead of twice daily

faq.question: I've tried PMR a couple of times, but I don't like it because I dislike the feeling of tightening my muscles.
faq.answer: This happens occasionally. In MiCBT, the PMR technique we use emphasizes the contrast between tension and relaxation. Highlighting this difference makes it easier to detect tension and release it more effectively during daily life. If this contrast is not sufficiently clear during practice, s benefits may not carry over as strongly into the rest of your day. Another reason tensing is helpful is that during periods of anxiety or high stress, we can feel a little disconnected from the body. At these times, tensing can help us feel grounded in the body. That said, if deliberately tensing muscles feels uncomfortable or isn't pos sible, there s an alternative method of practicing PMR in MiCBT that is also very helpful. Instead of a strong contraction, bring your attention to each muscle group following the same sequence and gently contract the muscles the contraction might be just at the threshold of awareness and then allow relaxation, to whatever degree is possible. This approach can be very benefi cial for managing subtle tension and stress throughout the day. Proceeding with the Next Chapter The most effective way to approach this book is to read one chapter each week. Begin reading Week 2 when you are ready to start the practice of mindfulness of breath.
