Aligning your reading with your practice prevents unnecessary confusion that might arise from reading too far ahead and allows for a natural balance between intellectual understanding and experi ential learning. By pacing yourself, you ll build a strong foundation for the skills and insights that follow. Happy practicing!
faq.keywords: ive tried pmr a couple of times but i dont like it because i dislike the feeling of tightening my muscles

faq.question: I get caught in memories of old girlfriends when we had a good time, and my imagination goes wild. I can't help having strong desires for sex when I practice. It s like I'm obsessed or something.
faq.answer: Ah craving! What you're experiencing is neither abnormal nor obsessive. Your practice is simply reflecting what is stored in your memory and how it is stored. When these memories arise, they often trig ger corresponding sensations in the body. This can be a warm tingling in the abdomen or face, a sense of pressure, or temperature changes in vari ous areas, or in any number of ways. Interestingly, this process can work both ways. In future practices, see whether certain sensations in the body are then followed by these memories emerging in the mind. Sometimes it's the memory that generates the body sensation, while other times it's the body sensation that triggers the memory. Table 2.2 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. Day Date MorningPractice(circle) Duration(minute) Response inhibitionrate from 1 to 10 how frequently you were able to notice dis tractions and bring attention back to the breath. 1 = once or twice; 10 = almost all the time Eveningpractice(circle) Duration(minutes) Response inhibitionrate from 1 to 10 how frequently you were able to notice dis tractions and bring attention back to the breath. 1 = once or twice; 10 = almost all the time Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/NoWednesday Yes/No Yes/NoThursday Yes/No Yes/NoFriday Yes/No Yes/NoSaturday Yes/No Yes/NoSunday Yes/No Yes/No 76 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE s possible that you ve recently experienced conscious or subconscious sexual cravings, and these sensations remained just beneath your conscious awareness until your practice allowed you to perceive them. And here s something to explore further: could it be that it is actually the body sensations that you crave, rather than sex itself? Try to observe these phenomena as objectively as possible during your practice, approaching even the experience of craving itself with equanimity, so that you make good use of your cravings! That said, don't spend too long on body sensations this week. We ll shift to mindfulness of body sensations next week. For now, observe the experi ence of craving briefly, noticing how it is experienced in the body and the mind, and then let it be and return your attention to the breath as soon as possible. Abandoning attachment to craving through single-pointed atten tion on your breath is a skillful approach to practice this week.
faq.keywords: i get caught in memories of old girlfriends when we had a good time and my imagination goes wild i cant help having stro

faq.question: I've been getting a strong pain in my lower back during prac tice. I wonder if it's related to an accident I had 15years ago. I had thought it had gotten better, but it seems that this practice is bringing the pain back. ve tried to work on my posture, but it's not going away, and now I'm find ing I don't want to practice. I've kept practicing twice a day, but I'm afraid s going to get worse!
faq.answer: It may sound strange to say well done, but it's good to know that you practiced even with the discomfort. That takes courage and commitment! Now, about your concern: mindfulness practice doesn't cause or bring on back pain. Rather, it helps you become aware of what s already present in your body. The practice itself doesn't create sensations; it brings to light sen sations that may have been just under the surface of your awareness. In other words, it allows you to feel what is already there. For your peace of mind, you might consider checking with your doctor to rule out any medical issues. At this early stage of your practice, if the uncomfortable sensations are manageable, the key is to not give them too much significance. Continue practicing calmly, without attaching negative judgments to the sensations. Feel the discomfort with equanimity, and then systematically return your attention to the breath. By fully re-engaging attention with the breath, you can avoid getting caught in stories or judgments about the sensations. If the sensations are intense to the extent that you cannot focus on the breath, then bring your attention to the area of most intense sensation for 30 seconds to a minute. Observe the sensations with curiosity and openness, noticing their qualities without taking them personally. These sensations are just transient experiences arising in the body they are not your pain. They are impermanent, with a beginning, a middle, and an end. Research suggests that focusing on the area of discomfort with equanimity can significantly reduce the intensity of unpleasantness within 30seconds! [33, 34] We ll explore this technique further in Week WEEK 2: REGULATING ATTENTION 77 As you practice, remind yourself that these sensations do not define you. They are not permanent and do not belong to you. Instead, they are simply arising in this moment. With time and patient observation, you may find your relationship with the discomfort shifting, even if the sensations them selves persist. Remember, everything changes, including these sensations.
faq.keywords: ive been getting a strong pain in my lower back during prac tice i wonder if its related to an accident i had 15years ag

faq.question: I don't really believe mindfulness practice is for me. My friends told me so many good things about this practice, but I don't think I can do it. And honestly, I feel guilty sitting down twice a day for 30minutes when I have so many things to do.
faq.answer: Doubt is a common theme when we start this practice. It typi cally falls into one of three categories: doubt about the technique, doubt about the teacher, or doubt about oneself. It sounds like you may be dealing with self-doubt. Is this something you notice in other areas of your life? Our established beliefs often shape how we approach challenges, includ ing mindfulness. If you tend to lack self-confidence, this can affect your belief in your ability to succeed with the practice. But here s the good news: this could be an opportunity to work with and reduce self-doubt. To do this, ll need to be attentive to any thoughts of doubt and the body sensations that arise alongside them. Instead of reacting to these sensations, observe them with curiosity and kindness. Notice that thoughts of self-doubt often center on and This focus on the self can amplify feelings of inadequacy, as we can always find something to criticize about ourselves. It can be a real relief to shift from judging yourself to focusing on the breath, remaining kind and patient with yourself. Abandon all expectations about yourself during the practice. When self-critical thoughts arise, see them for what they are just mental phenomena, not truths about you. Let go of the view that these thoughts define you. You can make it a key focus of your mindfulness practice this week, as an experiment!
faq.keywords: i dont really believe mindfulness practice is for me my friends told me so many good things about this practice but i do

faq.question: Is being focused on something, like when I'm really absorbed in a task, the same as mindfulness?
faq.answer: It depends! While being mindful always involves attentiveness, not all attentiveness is mindfulness. For instance, a cat poised in front of a mouse hole or a sniper ready to shoot are both examples of being fully con centrated on the here and now. However, in these cases, the focus is driven by craving (catching the mouse) or aversion (fear of being attacked). This type of attention lacks the qualities of complete or right mindfulness. In mindfulness, attention is free from craving or aversion. For instance, when we focus on the breath during meditation, the aim is not to crave peace of mind or avoid unwanted thoughts, but rather to observe the experi ence as it is, without attachment. Being attentive can help us complete tasks 78 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE or remain alert, but mindfulness goes beyond that. It requires a state of openness, acceptance, and non-reactivity qualities that help us relate to our experiences without identifying with them.
faq.keywords: is being focused on something like when im really absorbed in a task the same as mindfulness

faq.question: I feel very tired, and I need to relax my back. I find it difficult to sit with my neck and back straight. Can I lie down during the practice?
faq.answer: On days when we feel tired and have low energy, lying down to practice can seem very enticing! However, especially when you're already tired, lying down tends to increase drowsiness even if you're not consciously aware of it. Your practice space might even turn into a snoring camp If you're feeling too tired or drowsy to practice, take a short rest. Having a 5- to 10- minute nap, followed by walking around to wake up, can help. Then, sit down to practice with refreshed energy. s also helpful to reflect on the desire to lie down. It may stem from a craving for relaxation and relief or an aversion to unpleasant body sensa tions. Avoidance can fool you if you're not vigilant. If you want the best and quickest results, we encourage you to practice in the way that mindfulness meditators have done for the past 2,500years. Unless there s a serious medi cal reason that prevents you from sitting, it's best to practice in a seated position.
faq.keywords: i feel very tired and i need to relax my back i find it difficult to sit with my neck and back straight can i lie down d

faq.question: My mind is all over the place at times. I still get caught up in thoughts for ages before I realize it is happening. How do you stay focused?
faq.answer: This is common, no need for alarm! That moment of recogniz ing that attention has been caught up in thinking is actually very valuable. Congratulate yourself for making the effort to notice that this has occurred, because this is an important skill in and of itself. The quickest way to train your mind is to have the clear intention and firm determination that you will return attention to your breath within 3seconds of noticing a thought arising. This is called the 3-second rule. You might have observed that it's easier to recognize a thought the moment it appears than after a few seconds, once you ve already become engaged with it. If you don't let go of the thought immediately, you can easily get carried away and lost in it once you have succumbed to the temptation of thinking, as thinking is surprisingly addictive. Thinking is addictive because we get attached to feeling the pleasant or unpleasant body sensations that co-emerge with certain thoughts and to the brain chemicals that the brain produces at the same time. For example, a very pleasant thought will co-emerge with a very pleasurable body sensation and the associated dopamine, which can be very reinforcing. We also iden tify with thoughts and feel that they are part of who we are. You may already have noticed that it's easier to let go of thoughts that t have an emotional charge. In contrast, emotionally charged thoughts often co-emerge with strong body sensations, capturing your attention and WEEK 2: REGULATING ATTENTION 79 making them harder to release. When this happens, try identifying where these sensations are in the body. Shift your attention to that area and observe the sensations equanimously. Focus there until the intensity subsides, and then gently return your attention to the breath. Cultivating tolerance and patience is also essential for working with thoughts. Remind yourself that these are just thoughts and all of them share the same fundamental quality: they arise and pass away, and none of them are permanent. Not getting what we want whether it's focus or calmness while remaining patient strengthens our ability to respond similarly in daily life. This happens because we use the same brain in both contexts. When we develop patience in meditation, it naturally carries over into everyday situations.
faq.keywords: my mind is all over the place at times i still get caught up in thoughts for ages before i realize it is happening how d

faq.question: During the meditation, I often ruminate over past issues with my ex, and I worry about what s going to happen in the future. I noticed that when I bring my attention to my breath, I immediately feel better. This week, I kept on doing this, but it's like my mind is obsessive, and these thoughts keep coming back. Should I keep doing the same thing? It doesn seem to be working.
faq.answer: Redirecting attention from an unhelpful mental state to a neutral object of attention such as the breath can be helpful in some contexts. However, this is not likely to address the deeper aspects of the problem, which is why it doesn't seem to be working. When you refocus on the breath, you may be avoiding the sensations that co-emerge with your rumination and worry. Until the intensity of these sensations diminishes through mind ful and equanimous attention, the associated thoughts are likely to persist. Think of a thought as a helium balloon attached to a hook by a rope. The hook represents the body sensations that come with challenging thoughts. Until the rope is untied, the balloon cannot float freely. Similarly, unhelpful thoughts may continue to resurface until the underlying sensations are acknowledged and released. When intrusive thoughts are persistent, interrupt your focus on the breath and redirect your attention to the area of strongest sensation in your body. Observe the sensations with curiosity and acceptance, recognizing them as body sensations with the same fundamental characteristics as a thought: they are impermanent, therefore they cannot belong to you or be part of who you are, and identifying with them and reacting to them only brings more suffering. In daily life, too, refocusing attention on the breath is better than worry ing, but it may also become a way of avoiding what s happening in your body. Instead, notice how thoughts arise alongside body sensations and how these sensations influence the mind. By observing this interplay, you can begin to untie the rope, allowing both the thoughts and sensations to sub side more naturally. 80 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE
faq.keywords: during the meditation i often ruminate over past issues with my ex and i worry about what s going to happen in the futur

faq.question: I keep thinking of my divorce and the kids, and I find it very difficult to focus after just a few seconds. I really prefer the PMR practice because I could be more present and less distracted. I'm not sure what s the best way to go about it, but I don't think I'm ready to move on to mindful ness of breath.
faq.answer: Since PMR didn t resolve the problem of thinking about difficult topics, continuing with it isn't likely to help you stay more present after the practice ends. In contrast, mindfulness practice is like wearing a good pair of glasses it helps you see clearly what is usually blurred. Through mind fulness, you begin to understand the natural tendencies of your mind. What you are beginning to notice during practice is that the mind is agi tated and scattered. You re beginning to see the process of thinking, rather than just the content of your thoughts. This is progress! Now, your task is to observe this mental state mindfully without resenting it for being the way it is. Simply acknowledge that this agitation is impermanent and will subside in time. Resisting or resenting it only prolongs the agitation. This habit of wishing things were different is what creates many of our challenges in life. We don't want them to be as they are. Mindfulness gives you the opportunity to change this habit of resenting internal experience and wishing it was different. Accepting your internal experience as it is, in some ways, is accepting parts of yourself. This alone can calm the mind both in meditation and in daily life. It would be helpful for you to continue practicing mindfulness of breath for a week, but this time all on your own, without the recorded audio instructions. Since you are already familiar with the instructions, practicing in silence allows you to focus fully on the breath without the potential dis traction or lullaby soothing effect of the recording, which might reduce vigilance. Practice with patience, tolerance for your experience, and discernment. This means keeping in mind that we are not seeking any particular result. We are simply observing the way things are the reality of the present moment as it manifests itself when you breathe in and out, from moment to moment accepting whatever we discover. ve mentioned concerns about the divorce and your children, which may be contributing to the restlessness. Children often find comfort in their parents emotional stability and calmness. This doesn't mean being free of agitation but learning to remain steady even amidst challenging thoughts. During practice, commit to focusing on the breath and nothing else, keep ing the mind stable despite the underlying agitation. As counterintuitive as it may seem, the more you allow the mind to stay with the breath, the sooner the agitation will begin to settle.
faq.keywords: i keep thinking of my divorce and the kids and i find it very difficult to focus after just a few seconds i really prefe

faq.question: Why is it important to move my attention during body-scanning in this particular order?
faq.answer: There are three reasons for this. The first is that scanning in the same order each time ensures you don't forget any areas of the body, espe cially as the mind can be so easily distracted. This is not a big issue this week because you are using the audio instructions, but it can become an issue next week, when you ll be practicing in silence. The second reason for scanning in this order is that our attention natu rally gravitates toward areas with obvious sensations, making it easy to neglect parts of the body that are difficult to feel. This can happen without us being consciously aware of it! If we neglect these subtle sensations, we miss out on opportunities to feel the very early signs of change in the body, such as the early signs of emotions. So, by scanning your body in the same order each time, you give equal attention to all areas, ensuring you don miss any, including those that don't have many sensations or that feel blank. This practice strengthens neural connections in the insula and somatosen sory cortex, which will help you feel more of your body over time. 120 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE The third reason for scanning in this particular order is to lay the ground work for the more advanced methods that we will soon learn. Unilateral scanning is the most basic body-scanning method and one that we will only use for 2 weeks before starting more advanced scanning techniques. In future weeks, you ll be learning techniques like sweeping, where you move your attention fluidly through the body in a single, continuous motion, eventually at the speed of your breath. This requires a vertical approach to scanning. While it's fine to move horizontally in small areas, such as the face, prioritizing vertical scanning now will make it easier to progress later.
faq.keywords: why is it important to move my attention during bodyscanning in this particular order

faq.question: How fast should I go to scan'the whole body?
faq.answer: This week, the audio instructions will guide you through one full body cycle during your 30-minute session. A body cycle means scan ning from the top of your head to the tips of your toes, and then back up again. The slower pace this week helps you become familiar with the prac tice and builds a foundation for mindfulness of body sensations. Next week, you ll begin scanning at your own pace, completing at least two body cycles in each 30-minute session.
faq.keywords: how fast should i go to scanthe whole body

faq.question: I can't feel body sensations. What am I looking for?
faq.answer: It s entirely normal, especially when starting body-scanning, to experience some parts of the body as blank, with no noticeable sensa tions. As mentioned earlier in the chapter, this will gradually change over time as the brain wires to feel sensations in these areas. At times, though, it can seem like you can't feel any sensations. This can happen when we expect body sensations to be special or distinct in some way. They are not! During body-scanning, we are simply observing ordi nary, everyday sensations as they arise. Anything you feel counts as a body sensation. This might be sensations of temperature, such as feeling warmth or coolness in some part of your body. It could be sensations associated with movement, such as the movement of your chest wall or abdomen when breathing. It could be sensations of heaviness or lightness, such as feeling heaviness in the neck and other places due to fatigue. Sensations could also be related to the experience of density, such as feeling a sense of expansion or looseness when we are relaxed or joyful. We practice to observe our experiences in the body just as they are. If you t feel a particular area of your body, simply accept that you can't feel sensations in this particular part at this moment. Keep attention on that area for up to 10seconds. Bring an equal interest to each sensation, whether pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral, without taking it personally. As your ability to sustain attention and not get lost in thoughts improves week to week, ll be able to stay focused on blank spots more easily for up to 30seconds before moving on. WEEK 3: UNDERSTANDING AND REGULATING EMOTIONS 121 Remember: sensation or not, keep smiling and move on. This attitude of patience and acceptance cultivated during meditation carries over into daily life. When you don't get what you want or encounter challenges, you ll find yourself more tolerant, less agitated, and better equipped to respond skill fully. After all, the brain you use for meditation is the brain you also use in daily life. How you shape your brain during practice directly influences how it responds to everyday situations.
faq.keywords: i cant feel body sensations what am i looking for

faq.question: I've had strong feelings of sadness, frustration, and anxiety dur ing my last few practices. Scanning through them in the body helped to resolve them a little, but they creep back. There was a recent incident involv ing my eight-year-old son that upset me, and even though the issue is now resolved, I still feel overly emotional about it.
faq.answer: It sounds like you are genuinely trying to remain objective and equanimous. This is a great opportunity to further develop your equanim ity. First, it's important to acknowledge that your emotional reaction is entirely normal most people would react strongly if something were to happen to their child! Recognizing this can help you accept your experi ence with greater self-compassion. At the same time, your lingering dis tress suggests that while the external situation is resolved, thinking about it is still creating distress internally. Unresolved emotions consist of related thoughts or memories and their co-emerging body sensations. In your case, the sensations are co-emerging with memories of the incident involving your son, not the incident itself. Keeping this distinction in mind can help you bring genuine equanimity to the sensations. Your growing ability to feel body sensations means that you're becoming more aware of subtle sensations in daily life that you might not usually notice. This is similar to using a magnifying glass with enough magnifica tion, even the pores on your skin can look like large holes! The more you refine your awareness of subtle sensations, the more those that were only mildly uncomfortable seem more intense. You need to ensure that you create a good balance between interoceptive awareness (which serves as your mag nifying glass) and equanimity i.e., a balance between feeling and accepting body sensations. This will not only soothe your emotional experience, but it will also increase your ability to feel even subtler sensations during practice. If you're finding it challenging to sustain equanimity, try pausing your body-scanning practice briefly. Instead, focus on the area where sensations feel most intense. Rather than resisting or wishing the sensations away, examine the area of intensity carefully. Ask yourself: Do the sensations feel heavy or light? Do they move or are they still? What temperature do they have, if any? Are they dense or more diffuse? Keep attention on the area for 122 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE 60seconds and notice its changing nature, while accepting it fully. Doing this is likely to assist you in your effort to manage your sadness.
faq.keywords: ive had strong feelings of sadness frustration and anxiety dur ing my last few practices scanning through them in the bo

faq.question: The scanning is going well, but I still can't feel some parts of my chest and stomach, and I get frustrated. Am I doing something wrong?
faq.answer: Not at all. It s perfectly normal not to feel sensations in every part of the body at this stage. There are two main reasons for this. First, you may have noticed that it's much easier to feel sensations in the face, hands, and feet than in the torso. This is because there are more brain cells dedicated to feeling the face, hands, and feet than there are for the chest and abdomen. Second, it's natural to dislike unpleasant sensations in the abdomen or chest, such as those that occur with anxiety or chronic stress. Over time, avoiding these sensations can'turn down the volume of all sensations in those areas, making them harder to detect [74]. For this reason, it can'take a little longer to feel sensations in the front part of the torso compared to other parts of the body. Remember, not feeling sensations in some parts of the body is also impermanent; it will change with consistent practice. When you encounter blank area, try to maintain your attention calmly and equanimously on the blank area, as learning to be patient when we can't feel sensations is also part of the practice. However, if you practice twice daily and find that larger areas remain blank for several days, then focus within those blank spots by expanding your attention to cover a wider area at a time, about 3 4 in. in diameter, for around 30 seconds. Increasing the size of the area you are paying attention to will increase the chances of feeling a sen sation. Move on when you feel something or after approximately six to eight breaths.
faq.keywords: the scanning is going well but i still cant feel some parts of my chest and stomach and i get frustrated am i doing some

faq.question: I feel agitated when I practice. Is that normal?
faq.answer: Yes, experiencing agitation or restlessness is common when we first start the practice. It s nothing to be concerned about. In fact, this may be a valuable opportunity to carefully observe where sensations associated with agitation are experienced. Hold your attention there while remaining calm and equanimous for a minute or two. Investigate, dissect, and study what you witness with curiosity and genuine interest. Are they warm or cool? Still or moving? These sensations are impermanent, and just like everything else in life, they are not here to stay. s also important to remind yourself that the experience of agitation is just that: an experience. It doesn't define you, and it's not something you need to call or mine. Remember this each time you start to become agitated about the agitation! If you observe the experience in an equani mous way without identifying with it, it won't take long before the agitation transforms into something else. WEEK 3: UNDERSTANDING AND REGULATING EMOTIONS 123 If the agitation is caused by persistent thoughts, instead of focusing on the thoughts or their co-emerging sensations, focus on the breath and nothing else, keeping your mind as stable as possible despite the underlying agita tion. Once your mind has settled, return to scanning the body. You might find it helpful to remember the acronym FAME: Feel, Accept, and Move with Equanimity, which was created by one of our clients. This can serve as a simple reminder of how to approach sensations and the atti tude to bring to them during your practice.
faq.keywords: i feel agitated when i practice is that normal

faq.question: I've noticed that my eyes move to the area of the body I focusing on. For example, when I try to feel the top of my head, my eyes look up. When I scan my left shoulder, my eyes look toward that shoulder. Basically, I scan with my eyes, too! It s very tiring, and I get a small head ache. Is it ok for my eyes to be moving like this?
faq.answer: This is a common experience when starting body-scanning. Many of us instinctively use our visual modality to support the move ment of attention by visualizing the body parts we're focusing on. This can lead to unintentional eye movements, as the brain is more accustomed to relying on vision than on interoception (the ability to sense internal body sensations). When we can't easily feel certain areas of the body, the brain tries to them instead. Doing this for 30minutes can easily lead to a headache. Bruno recalls experiencing the same thing when he began practicing over 35years ago. The sooner you stop tracking your sensations with your eyes, the more com fortable and effective your practice will become. As your ability to feel sensations develops, the habit of trying to also them will naturally decrease. For now, simply notice when your eyes begin to move and gently rest them in one position. Focus on scanning the body without engaging your eyes. If this feels challenging, try opening your eyes slightly and resting your gaze on a fixed point on the floor a short distance in front of you while continuing to scan'the body.
faq.keywords: ive noticed that my eyes move to the area of the body i focusing on for example when i try to feel the top of my head my

faq.question: It s very strange, when I started this practice last week, I began to feel some nausea during body-scanning. Not too much, but enough to feel uncomfortable. Is this normal? Is it possible that mindfulness could be harmful for some people?
faq.answer: No, being mindful is not harmful to people, but nausea does happen sometimes, and it passes. It can also happen during progressive muscle relaxation and mindfulness of breath, often as we become more aware of our emotions or worry about them. Remember, nausea doesn last forever, and, like other forms of discomfort, it is a good tool for devel oping equanimity. In fact, you couldn t develop equanimity without dis comfort. Just as we can only learn patience when we are impatient, we can only learn equanimity when we are reactive. WEEK 3: UNDERSTANDING AND REGULATING EMOTIONS 125 s also useful to remember that nausea can arise in the context of a variety of conditions, both mental and physical, including anxiety, depres sion, post-traumatic stress disorder, eating disorders, migraines, infection, food poisoning, or medication side effects, which sometimes occur when medication is taken on an empty stomach. Even dietary changes, such as eating too quickly or consuming spicy or fatty foods, can'trigger nausea. If nausea persists outside of meditation and doesn't seem directly related to it, it may be a good idea to consult your doctor to rule out any underlying health issues. s important to understand that the practice of sitting calmly and scan ning the body does not inherently cause nausea. Instead, it may increase your awareness of subtle sensations linked to residual anxiety, which can manifest as nausea. Alternatively, a past memory of nausea might be re triggered during practice. When nausea arises, do your best to remain equanimous, reminding yourself that the discomfort is temporary and not dangerous in and of itself. If the sensations are too overwhelming during meditation, even after focusing equanimously on the four characteristics mass, motion, temperature, and density in the area where it is most intense for about half a minute, then focus calmly on the breath, continuing with mindfulness of breath for the rest of that session. If you find that the nausea increases or other intense experiences arise in the following few days, stop practicing body scanning and replace it with PMR for a few sessions. If you find that nausea has subsided and there is no need for a medical checkup, resume body scanning.
faq.keywords: it s very strange when i started this practice last week i began to feel some nausea during bodyscanning not too much bu

faq.question: I've been having headaches during the body-scanning, usually after about 15 20minutes every time I practice. I didn t experience this dur ing PMR or Mindfulness of Breath. I would appreciate your thoughts on what might be causing them.
faq.answer: There could be several factors contributing to the headaches. s common for people to put a lot of effort into trying to feel sensations, which can lead to tension headaches. If you find yourself in this situation, take a step back, abandon'the strong desire to feel something specific, and simply notice what sensations are arising naturally, if any. s also worth considering whether the headaches come more frequently when you are scanning certain parts of the body. At times, some body sensa tions during our practice can feel similar to those we feel during stressful situations, such as past or present conflicts, painful memories, relationship or work dissatisfaction, and other issues we may not feel prepared to face at this moment, perhaps because of fears that they will be opening a can of worms, so to speak. The emotion we may not yet be ready to feel can still be subconscious, and we therefore can't yet make sense of it. It s natural to automatically, often subconsciously, attempt to suppress these emotions, as they often feel uncomfortable. Has there been any old issue intensifying or 126 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE any new issue arising in your life? Avoiding these sensations during medita tion can create much tension, which can result in headaches. Because this process often occurs at a largely subconscious level, do your best to remind yourself that the headache is simply an unpleasant sensation that will pass in time. Resisting or wishing it away can inadvertently increase its intensity, as tension and worry tend to amplify discomfort. The more relaxed and equanimous you remain during practice, the more likely the headache will dissipate naturally and with greater ease.
faq.keywords: ive been having headaches during the bodyscanning usually after about 15 20minutes every time i practice i didn t experi

faq.question: I cannot feel ordinary sensations clearly unless I visualize each area of my body. Is there something wrong with my brain?
faq.answer: There is nothing wrong with your brain. Where you can't feel a sensation, simply acknowledge this without judgment this acceptance helps maintain a calm and focused mind. Stay with each blank spot for up to 30 seconds, and then move on to the next part of your body, whether or not a sensation becomes noticeable. This approach reduces the ten dency to visualize body parts. If, after three days, you still feel that there is little to no progress, consider switching to Mindfulness of Breath for the remainder of the week. This will help strengthen your ability to regulate attention, making it easier to sustain focus on areas with subtle or faint sensations when you return to body scanning the following week. Finally, let go of any judgments about your ability or performance. Rather than expecting to feel sensations because the audio instructions suggest it, simply notice whether you can feel sensations in a part of the body or not. Your worth is not defined by your ability to perceive body sensations. Skillful practice is characterized by making an effort to feel sensations while approaching your experience without reacting to it or identifying with it in other words, practicing with equanimity.
faq.keywords: i cannot feel ordinary sensations clearly unless i visualize each area of my body is there something wrong with my brain

faq.question: For the past few days, during meditation, I've been obsessing about roast pork. I can hear the pork ribs crackling in the oven, and I can even smell the exquisite aroma in my mind. And I just can't help it, after my meditation, I have to cook something.
faq.answer: Cravings, especially for familiar things like food, are common during body-scanning and can be easily explained. Remember how we discussed in Week 2 that spontaneous thoughts are likely informed by our recent and frequent thoughts, or previous emotionally charged experiences? To revisit these ideas, the frequency effect refers to habitual thoughts that arise because of their prominence in daily life. For instance, if you often think about food, these thoughts are more likely to arise during meditation, especially during moments when your concentration dips. The co-emergence effect, on the other hand, happens when body sensa tions during meditation resemble sensations linked to past experiences. For example, if you consciously or subconsciously feel a pleasant body sensation similar to one you ve felt when you were craving roast pork in the past, it acts as a memory cue, triggering the memory of craving roast pork. But this is only a memory of craving. It s not craving in and of itself it only becomes craving if you react to the memory by engaging with it, effectively making the past your present. During body-scanning, rather than focusing on the sensory memories themselves such as the image of roast pork or hearing it crackle redirect your attention to the pleasurable sensations that co-emerge with the memo ries. For example, when a pleasant memory of the smell of roast pork arises, shift your attention to the co-emerging pleasant body sensations and feel them equanimously, rather than focusing on the smell. Continue to feel the pleasant sensations, but without attachment to them or craving more. Just observe them with genuine curiosity for a little while, like a scientist observ ing the mechanisms of an experiment and collecting data. Which of the four characteristics is predominant: mass, motion, temperature, or density? Or is 164 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE it perhaps a combination of these? See how impermanent they are when you remain aware and equanimous. The trap of being attached to sensations that are changing all the time will become clear because of past attachment to them, we crave them when they are not present, and we remain unfulfilled. Through your practice of awareness and equanimity during meditation, you can learn to neutralize your cravings, which in turn will assist you in addressing craving-related behaviors in your daily life, like emotional eating, with greater freedom and understanding.
faq.keywords: for the past few days during meditation ive been obsessing about roast pork i can hear the pork ribs crackling in the ov

faq.question: I still can't feel many sensations in my face when I scan, so I have been imagining the shape of my nose, cheeks, and ears to try to feel more. Is it okay to do that?
faq.answer: It s very common to need more time to feel the whole body. When we can't feel certain areas, the brain often automatically compen sates by visualizing those parts this occurs spontaneously. The brain is remarkable in its ability to problem-solve. When one function isn't fully developed, like sensing certain body parts, another function, such as visu alization, will come to the rescue. When you scan'the body, you activate nerve cells contained in the soma tosensory cortex, a strip of the brain responsible for processing sensations. Another region, the insula, becomes involved when scanning sensations linked to emotions. If the neural connections between some groups of cells in the somatosensory cortex and insula aren t yet well-developed, there may be parts of the body that are still difficult to feel. For example, if the neurons that allow you to feel your ears aren t sufficiently connected with each other, it might be hard to feel your ears. When this happens, the visual cortex will activate to help you imagine their shape. However, relying on visualization strengthens the neural pathways for seeing rather than feeling the body. If our goal was simply to become more attentive and concentrated, more focused in the present moment, then visu alizing parts of the body and using verbal cues, like mantras, would cer tainly be helpful and welcome during our practice. But in MiCBT, our purpose is very different. We have a very strong rationale for training our selves to feel the body. We train to feel body sensations to cultivate equanim ity, not just concentration. In this way, we learn to not react with more craving and aversion toward sensations associated with emotions. Through this, we regulate both attention and emotions. If this resonates with you, do your best to just notice the blank spots when you scan'the body and prevent craving or aversion from arising. Pay atten tion there for 30seconds to a minute, or until you feel a sensation. When you move, do so smilingly, so to speak, without impatience, disappoint ment, or frustration. This is part of normal practice and is not a reason to feel defeated or incompetent. week 4: applying mindfulness skills in daily life 165 For now, accept that this is how it is at this moment, and that this, too, is impermanent. Over time, you ll likely be able to feel every part of your body, except areas like hair or the outer nails. In the bigger picture, not feeling sensations when you scan'the body is an opportunity to work on your patience and equanimity, so that you ll be able to do the same in daily life when things don't go as planned.
faq.keywords: i still cant feel many sensations in my face when i scan so i have been imagining the shape of my nose cheeks and ears t

faq.question: I have serious pain in my knee when I practice, even though I sitting on a chair, not even cross-legged on the floor! I injured my knee at the gym about 5years ago. Do you think the body-scanning is making it worse? What should I do about it?
faq.answer: Body-scanning doesn't create pain; it only reveals what is already there. Think of it like washing dirty clothes the dirt comes out, but it was in the clothes, not caused by the soap. Similarly, body-scanning is like soap; it allows you to notice existing sensations, but it doesn't cause them. The intense sensations you're feeling during practice were already there, or came up during practice, but not because of the practice. It s common to feel the physical memory of old injuries when you start body-scanning, and this tends to decrease quickly over time. However, if you're concerned about an underlying physical issue, it's always a good idea to consult your doctor. In the meantime, remember our discussion regarding the four basic char acteristics of body sensations. Since what we call pain is simply composed of intense sensations, it can be addressed with equanimity. Focus your atten tion on the most intense spot in your knee, staying with it as if you're in the calm eye of a storm, patiently and equanimously, for up to 30seconds. Which of the four characteristics is most predominant? Perhaps constric tion? Or heat? Observe what happens when you genuinely allow and wel come the experience, rather than resisting or resenting it. If you find it difficult to tolerate the sensation, it may be because you identifying too closely with it, believing that this is your sensation and therefore your body, and, consequently, your pain. Even if you're very committed to practicing in absolute immobility, gritting your teeth and sweating it out while resenting or hating the pain will only increase fear and aversion, and your mental state will be anything but equanimous. It s like trying to break a stone with an egg; it simply doesn't work! Remember that equanimity needs to be applied to all reactive habits, including unhelpful thoughts. Recognizing that this is just a thought or this is just a sensa tion helps minimize your identification with the experience, making it eas ier to remain equanimous even when it is very intense. Ensure that you relax into the four basic characteristics (mass, motion, temperature, and density) of the intense sensation and let those characteris tics just be. Do your best to feel and accept them unconditionally. If the pain becomes too intense, then have a rest by moving your leg a little or have a small break from the practice and return to it a little later. 166 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE By using intense sensations like pain to develop equanimity, you put the difficult experience to good use. It becomes an opportunity to develop patience and determination, and can lead to rapid changes in daily life. You turn potential suffering into happiness capital, and even pain can become acceptable when you know it's not wasted and may even benefit you in the future.
faq.keywords: i have serious pain in my knee when i practice even though i sitting on a chair not even crosslegged on the floor i inju

faq.question: With the Mindfulness based Interoceptive Signature Scale (Figure 4.1) that I'm using this week to record those four characteristics, how do I fill it in if there is an issue during my work meeting tomorrow? I t exactly tell my boss, Wait a minute, I have to record my sensations on this form! How can I fill it out in a timely way?
faq.answer: Yes, this can be a little tricky sometimes, especially in situations like work meetings or driving, when it is not practical. Whether you using the pen-and-paper format or the MiCBT Guide app, you may not 170 STAGE 1 PERSONAL STAGE always be able to record your sensations on the spot. In these cases, do your best to make a mental note of how the sensations feel in the body in terms of the four characteristics. Then, as soon as you have the opportu nity, record your experience on the form or in the app.
faq.keywords: with the mindfulness based interoceptive signature scale figure 41 that im using this week to record those four characte

faq.question: Do I have to record all my emotions with the MISS this week? My God you have no idea I will need 50 sheets like this one!
faq.answer: Ha ha! This won't be necessary with every emotion you feel. You just need to monitor the stronger emotions because they are the ones with which you can best develop equanimity. Whether you're using the MiCBT app or using pen and paper, fill in the MISS as often as possible when you feel distressed.
faq.keywords: do i have to record all my emotions with the miss this week my god you have no idea i will need 50 sheets like this one

faq.question: How fast should I scan my body with symmetrical scanning?
faq.answer: Move at your own pace. When you encounter blank areas in the body places where sensations aren t noticeable t interrupt your flow. Continue scanning through the whole body symmetrically to complete the cycle you started. Afterward, return to each blank spot one at a time, focus ing equanimously for about 30 60seconds on each area. If a sensation arises during this time, observe it equanimously, noticing how even blank spots can change, and then shift attention to the next blank spot and do the same. If no change occurs, don't be disappointed or you will lose your equanim ity. Instead, accept that this is not the time for it to change. Not getting the result you expect in meditation while staying equanimous will be of great benefit when you can't get what you want in daily life; you will be able to remain calm and equanimous.
faq.keywords: how fast should i scan my body with symmetrical scanning

faq.question: I can't feel some body parts symmetrically. What should I do?
faq.answer: That s very common. Don t expect to feel both sides perfectly symmetrically for the first few days. There will be a lead between sides when scanning certain areas of the body, causing your attention to quickly shift back and forth from one side to the other very rapidly to catch up. This happens because sensations are easier to feel symmetrically in some areas of the body and more difficult in others. This is to be expected. As you maintain your commitment to practice for 30minutes twice daily, it will only take a few days before new and stronger neural connections are made in the relevant brain areas and you are able to feel the whole body symmetri cally, without having to flick your attention quickly from side to side. Remaining immobile and equanimous will help.
faq.keywords: i cant feel some body parts symmetrically what should i do

faq.question: I avoid many things due to my OCD, but anything dirty, like dusty things, is my biggest problem. Should I write a hierarchy of situations just focus ing on these fears, or should I try to include a variety of different situations?
faq.answer: It s fine to include several situations related to the same family of problems, such as two or three increasingly anxiety-provoking situa tions involving dust. However, it's also important to expose yourself to a larger variety of avoided situations. The reason for this is that it's more Table 5.5 Daily record of mindfulness meditation practice. Day Date Morningpractice(circle) Duration (minutes) Equanimity ratingRate from 1 to 10 your ability to stay equanimous while feeling body sensations. 1 = almost never; 10 = almost always Evening practice(circle) Duration (minutes) Equanimity ratingRate from 1 to 10 your ability to stay equanimous while feeling body sensations. 1 = almost never; 10 = almost always Monday Yes/No Yes/No Tuesday Yes/No Yes/NoWednesday Yes/No Yes/NoThursday Yes/No Yes/NoFriday Yes/No Yes/NoSaturday Yes/No Yes/NoSunday Yes/No Yes/No 198 STAGE 2 EXPOSURE STAGE beneficial to your self-confidence if you experience relief across a variety of fears, rather than just becoming confident enough that you no longer need to wear gloves around dust. If you recall our discussions and your experiences with mindfulness, you're not actually avoiding the dust. You avoiding the unpleasant body sensations you feel when you think you going to be contaminated. As your brain learns that all sensations are manageable and none is a threat and therefore there is no need to react whatever you use for exposure in the outside world will help you progress, both in relation to contamination fears and in other areas of avoidance.
faq.keywords: i avoid many things due to my ocd but anything dirty like dusty things is my biggest problem should i write a hierarchy

faq.question: I really can't think of anything that I avoid. My problem is with my boss and the other staff who support him. That s why I've been on sick leave for so long, but I'm not someone who avoids things avoidance isn really an issue for me.
faq.answer: Sometimes, the language we use can shape how we view our situation. If you say to yourself that the cause of your three-year workers compensation claim is that people were rude to you and took advantage of you for so long, then the problem doesn't seem to have anything to do with you. It can even seem like the problem lies entirely outside of your control. At the same time, we can be curious about why you waited until the situation became unbearable before addressing it. Were you perhaps avoiding conflict, embarrassment, or even your own anger or fear? ve also mentioned that you ve decided not to return to that job, so why rely solely on the workers compensation process rather than exploring new opportunities that might suit you better? Could it be that you're avoid ing taking healthy risks? Avoidant behavior isn't just about the things we t do, it can also be reflected in the choices we make. If you're struggling to identify situations that you avoid, consider asking someone close to you, like a partner, sibling, or friend. They might offer insights into your behaviors or decision-making that you hadn t recognized as avoidant.
faq.keywords: i really cant think of anything that i avoid my problem is with my boss and the other staff who support him that s why i

faq.question: I think I'm starting to really get the whole avoidance thing, during bipolar exposure, I don't understand why I need to visualize the 200 STAGE 2 EXPOSURE STAGE best-case scenarios as well, since that s not what I am avoiding. Also, can you explain why it's necessary to visualize the best-case scenarios last rather than the worst-case ones?
faq.answer: When we face situations in real life that we ve been avoiding, the outcomes can vary. Things tend to go well, but sometimes they don There is no guarantee either way. While we often expect some degree of anxiety, we might also expect things to go well, building an expectation of success. But what happens if things don't go as well as we hoped? The disappointment could be difficult to manage, especially if we haven t yet learned to be with pleasant sensations with equanimity. Therefore, during bipolar exposure, we also visualize the best-case sce narios in order to allow pleasant body sensations to co-emerge while remain ing equanimous toward them. With practice, the attachment to pleasant outcomes decreases, and we are less likely to feel disappointed if real-life exposure doesn't go as expected. This also reduces any potential sense of failure and discouragement that can come from unmet expectations, allow ing us to continue with the practice. As for why we visualize the best-case scenarios last, and not first, this has to do with the processing of expected outcomes in memory. Do you remem ber our discussion of the recency effect during mindfulness of breath in Week 2? Our most recent experiences tend to be remembered more readily because brain cells that produce that memory are more readily activated. This applies to bipolar exposure as well. If we visualized the worst-case scenarios last, that would be what we remembered most between bipolar exposure sessions, and we might not be as confident to perform exposure in real life. By ending bipolar exposure with the pleasant scenarios, we leave ourselves with a positive impression of the forthcoming real-life exposure, which boosts our motivation and confidence.

faq.question: My partner keeps telling me that I avoid visiting her parents on weekends like we used to, but frankly, it's just because I d get angry with them and end up saying what I really think. So, I prefer to stay home and spare my partner the embarrassment. I don't think I'm avoiding anything by not wanting to hurt her feelings, though.
faq.answer: It sounds like you care a lot about your partner s feelings and her relationship with her parents. But why is it that you feel you can't visit them without reacting with anger? What is it about those visits that s hard to handle equanimously? Sometimes, we avoid situations where we might face embarrassing or invalidating comments, or where we feel disrespected or uncomfortable. In these situations, it's the unpleasant sensations we feel that we're really trying to avoid. Now, if the issue is best addressed through direct communication, it might be better to hold off until we get to Stage 3 of the program. That s when 202 STAGE 2 EXPOSURE STAGE ll dive into mindful interpersonal skills and how to handle difficult conversations effectively. So, if visiting your partner s parents means address ing unresolved issues, then it might be best to revisit this in 2weeks, when we start learning mindful interpersonal skills. On the other hand, if you could visit them while observing sensations associated with anger while maintaining equanimity without needing to address the underlying issues directly this could be valuable exposure practice for this stage.

faq.answer: At this stage, don't worry about how deep in the body you feeling sensations. It s perfectly fine to only feel sensations on the surface. faq.question: It was amazing! I had an out-of-body experience. I could float around the room and go wherever I wanted. I think this is called astral traveling. Is that an okay thing to do?
faq.answer: That must have been a fascinating experience! It s not uncom mon for meditators who reach certain levels of skill to encounter unusual phenomena. However, it's important to approach such experiences with care and understanding. This reminds me of a retreat I attended many years ago. I was amazed at the teacher s reply when someone with a simi lar experience showed great excitement. The teacher said, That s fine, but make sure you can still feel sensations in the body and remain equani mous. Initially, the meditator seemed a bit confused or even disappointed, but they later appreciated the importance of this advice. The key here is to approach such experiences with the right understanding. While acknowledging the uniqueness of these kinds of experiences, it's essen tial to treat them as impermanent experiences that can be a potential source of suffering if you cling to them. For instance, what if this kind of experience doesn't happen again? How disappointing! You might be inclined to chase after it and find yourself trying to have another similar experience. Focusing on the past with craving has us miss what is happening in the present moment and reinforces the mind s habit of craving pleasant experiences. If it does happen again spontaneously, simply observe it with equanimity. And if it doesn't, accept that as well. There are many more enriching moments that await you as your practice develops.

faq.question: I'm still not clear on why I can't just block my negative thoughts when they come up. I know you ve explained a lot about that, but it's still confusing for me. For example, when I do my mindfulness practice, I keep thinking about being summoned at any time for my court case. I just can get it out of my mind.
faq.answer: This is a common question! Most of us aren t used to accepting thoughts as just thoughts and letting them pass, whether this is during meditation or while practicing Right Effort, even though research shows that suppressing thoughts is not efficient, and usually not effective [6]. Would you like to try a short exercise to see why this might be? s give it a try! Lean back in your chair and close your eyes. Now, imagine a police officer knocking at your door and handing you a letter to appear in court. Take half a minute to picture this as vividly as you can Now, switch your attention to your breath and try not to think about the event at all. re laughing because you ve realized how difficult it is to stop thinking about something once it's already in your mind. This is partly because in order to block a thought, you have to first think about it! This keeps activating the week 6: improving self- confidence 219 same brain cells, which maintains the thought rather than getting rid of it! The harder you try to block it, the more it sticks around. The key is to remain equanimous and allow those neurons to deactivate in their own time. Otherwise, the only other option is to mask the thought by distracting yourself with an even stronger thought. For example, you might think about a fun memory from your last holiday to temporarily push away thoughts about the court summons. While this might work for a moment, it's not always practical especially in situations where distrac tions are hard to find like when you're in bed trying to fall asleep. In these cases, unwanted thoughts often persist, leading to rumination. You may have experienced this pattern yourself! Your question also highlights an important observation: when we try to block a thought but remain unaware of the body sensations that arise with it, the thought doesn't truly go away. Instead, it remains tied to the body sensations. Since the sensations are part and parcel of the thought, when one arises, the other does too. If we're unaware of the sensations, it becomes harder to approach them equanimously, making the associated thoughts even more difficult to release. So, when an unwanted thought arises, try this instead: focus your full attention on the body sensations that come with it. Stay with those sensa tions quietly, peacefully, and equanimously. As the emotional intensity of the thought decreases, you ll notice that the thought itself becomes less persistent. Attempting to block persisting, troubling thoughts won't make them go away; it can even make them more persistent. But acknowledging, under standing, and accepting the associated body sensations is a more effective way to let go of these thoughts.

faq.question: I found part-by-part scanning easier because I didn t have so many thoughts. But now that I've started the sweeping method, I'm getting caught up in even more thoughts than in the previous 2 weeks. I think I going backward and losing the progress I made. What am I doing wrong? What should I do?
faq.answer: It s understandable to feel concerned, but the good news is that this isn't a sign of regression. Remember our earlier discussions about not getting attached to impermanent mental states? This situation is a perfect example. When our experience changes, it's easy to feel dissatisfied if we become attached to a particular way of experiencing things. This is an important reflection on impermanence not just in meditation, but in life as a whole. Think about changes in your career or relationships; disap pointment often arises because we want things to stay the way we prefer. The same principle applies here. Now, let s address why you're noticing more thoughts during sweeping. Hopefully, this explanation will help you let go of the idea that you 220 STAGE 2 EXPOSURE STAGE regressing. When you practiced part-by-part body scanning, your focus was narrower and more defined. This is because you were feeling a smaller num ber of sensations at a time, so there were fewer sensations for thoughts to co-emerge with! With sweeping, however, you're covering much larger areas of the body in a given period, and this naturally brings up more sensations. As a result, more thoughts are likely to co-emerge with those sensations. This is normal and is why most people experience the return of persistent, intrusive thoughts when they practice using sweeping techniques. The key is to recognize that these thoughts are not a problem. They arise because of the increased flow of sensations being observed s simply a natural part of the process. Rather than feeling frustrated, focus on staying equanimous toward both the thoughts themselves and any expectations that it should be different. What a wonderful set of skills to have in daily life!

faq.question: It s really unlike me, but I missed my practice this morning. It the first time I've done only 13 sessions this week instead of 14. I don't think s a big problem. Or is it?
faq.answer: Congratulations on being so dedicated to your practice! 13 out of 14 sessions is still an excellent commitment and remains very productive provided your practice is accurate. In fact, you're the only client we can recall in many years of teaching this program who has con sistently practiced every session up until this week. Actually, we're glad you didn t practice this morning. Let me explain why. One of the key reasons you started this program was to address perfec tionism and performance anxiety, which are often rooted in a fear of failure. Growing up in a strict family, you had to obey your parents to the letter for fear of the consequences. In the past, missing even a single session might have triggered stress or anxiety. But from your demeanor today, that doesn seem to be the case. This is wonderful progress! At the micro level of experience, your increased equanimity is reducing reactivity to body sensations associated with concerns about performance. As a result, at the macro level, your overall attitude toward achievement and how others evaluate you is also changing. This reflects meaningful change well done! That said, it's still true that the more consistently you practice, the more things will change for the better. After all, it's your daily dedicated practice which has started to free you from these fears. So while missing one session isn't a problem, it's the continued dedication to practice that will help you sustain and deepen these changes.

faq.question: Can you clarify what I'm supposed to do during the 1-minute rest period during bipolar exposure am I just supposed to focus on the breath? And when I imagine the worst- and best-case scenarios, am I sup posed to focus on the four characteristics of body sensations or just on the scenarios themselves?
faq.answer: Great questions! First, during the 1-minute rest period, you just need to attend to the breath, like you did during the mindfulness of breath meditation. Calmly focusing your attention fully on breath sensations at the nostrils will help you let go of any lingering thoughts and concerns related to imagining unpleasant scenarios. Whether to focus on the four characteristics of body sensations during imagined scenarios depends on the intensity of the situation. For the most distressing items on your list when imagining worst- and best-case scenarios s beneficial to focus on the obvious characteristics of the most intense body sensations. Observe these sensations with equanimity, as remaining equanimous is the cornerstone of the desensitization process for situations that evoke strong co-emerging sensations. This practice not only facilitates desensitization but also helps reduce the emotional charge of these scenarios over time. If you were to revisit the first or second item on your exposure list now, they would likely no longer evoke intense sensations. In this case, there s no week 6: improving self- confidence 223 need to carefully observe the characteristics of mass, motion, movement, or density simply allow the sensations to arise and accept them as they are.

faq.question: I thought I d be able to finish all five exposure tasks this week, but something came up, and it wasn t possible to do the last situation in real life. I know we're moving on to something else this coming week. Should I just drop this one?
faq.answer: The logistics of organizing exposures in daily life can be a little tricky sometimes. Schedules change, unexpected things happen, and some situations fall through. This is completely understandable. If possible, con sider replacing that last situation with another one that causes a near equivalent percentage of distress. If that s not feasible, choose a new situation that is as close as possible to the same distress level as the original task and practice with that instead. Since the effort required to maintain equanimity will be nearly the same, you ll still gain comparable benefits.

faq.question: My last situation on the list is my fear of contacting my daugh ter after 17years of us not talking. I really understand the practice and I did well with the other four situations, but I'm not confident that I can handle this one yet, even though I know I need to because of my cancer.
faq.answer: You ve built a strong foundation of mindfulness and equanimity, and you ve already applied these so successfully to some very challenging situations well done! You ve started to build your inner refuge. Remember, intense distress only arises when craving and aversion take over, and you allow yourself to identify with the experience. By staying 224 STAGE 2 EXPOSURE STAGE equanimous while not identifying with the sensations or the experience, you may still feel a mild degree of distress, but you will be able to let even that ll notice it passes much more quickly, just as it has throughout this training. Have faith in the skills you ve developed. Make an effort to approach this exposure task with patience and, above all, be kind to yourself. Facing your fear of rejection and shame may feel daunting, but allowing yourself to approach your daughter could also offer a meaningful opportunity to reconnect.

faq.question: When I go back to part-by-part scanning after two or three sweepings, do I need to scan'the whole body part by part or can I still sweep the parts that sweep easily? Or should I really break down the scanning for every part of the body?
faq.answer: When you go back to part-by-part scanning after two or three sweepings, focus only on the specific areas where you didn t feel sensa tions as readily, or in as much detail. You don't need to sweep through the rest of the body while doing this more targeted part-by-part scanning. Once you have addressed all the unclear areas using part-by-part scanning, then return to two or three cycles of sweeping en masse.

faq.question: Regarding the bipolar exposure method, the scenario I'm imag ining often doesn't feel real, in that it seems to be missing the emotional part of the experience. Is this a problem, or does it mean I am actually doing it correctly, I mean, with sufficient equanimity? 250 STAGE 3 INTERPERSONAL STAGE
faq.answer: This is fairly common, and it can happen for several reasons. As you suggest, one possibility is that you ve already developed a significant level of equanimity. If so, the scenario may no longer provoke noticeable emotions and their associated arousal, and you ll likely be able to face the situation in real life with increased confidence. Another reason could be difficulty evoking emotions using imagination. Some people find it challenging to evoke strong feelings when visualizing future events, even though they may readily recall emotions associated with past experiences. If this describes you, try imagining the scenario happening in the next hour or two, which might make it feel more immediate and real. To help create these sensations, ensure you genuinely believe you will actu ally be facing the situation in the next few hours. s also possible that the situation you selected isn't as distressing as you initially thought. For instance, if you rated your third situation as causing 50% distress but it actually only causes 35% distress, it won't generate much arousal because you ve already exposed and worked through your second situation and reduced your distress at that 35% level. If this is the case, choose a more challenging situation to work with. Alternatively, your brain may have learned to avoid feeling certain types of emotional discomfort, which is quite common. Visualizing a situation that could evoke these strong sensations may result in your brain inhibiting these emotional signals in the body, making it harder to feel them. If this is the case, this particular situation may need to be rated more highly on your list and addressed later, once your equanimity has grown, as the sensations associ ated with it are potentially much stronger than you initially realized. In the meantime, choose a different situation with a similar distress level. For exam ple, if you had initially rated this situation as 60% distressing, but it's actu ally 80%, choose another 60% situation to work with. Working with this new item will still produce the growth in equanimity you are looking for.

faq.question: One of the situations I've been avoiding is chatting with my boss at work. faq.answer: Yes, that s often how we use it. Using bipolar exposure before practicing experiential ownership in real life is a helpful way to prepare, especially for situations you would typically avoid. During bipolar expo sure, you can practice the steps of experiential ownership in imagination during both the worst- and best-case scenarios and familiarize yourself with the thoughts and sensations that might come up when you interact with her in person. This preparation can be particularly useful for complex interpersonal situations like the one you ve described with your boss, where there are WEEK 7: IMPROVING RELATIONSHIPS 251 boundary issues and heightened emotional triggers. Practicing in this way can help you feel more confident and centered when facing the situation in real life.

faq.question: _ I find it more difficult to scan'through the body with transversal scanning than when I sweep. And I really like the sweeping! Do I really have to use this method or can I continue with sweeping en masse?
faq.answer: It’s completely natural to prefer the sweeping method, as it can often feel quite pleasant as attention flows freely through the body. Transversal scanning, in contrast, involves going back to part-by-part scanning, which can feel more laborious and a bit like restarting from the beginning. This is an opportunity to examine the workings of attachment. Could it be that you’ve grown attached to the pleasant sensations—such as tingling or flowing—that often occur during sweeping en masse? And now, perhaps, you feel a sense of loss because those sensations are no longer there during transversal scanning? If so, this is a powerful reminder of how meditation mirrors the mind’s tendencies in daily life. By closely examining these reac- tions, you can gain deeper insight into how attachment ultimately creates discomfort and dissatisfaction, even during meditation. This is the story of our life! Now that you are asked to feel something else, the absence of that which had created pleasure has become the source of some frustration. Yet, every experience in life is ultimately impermanent, and sooner or later, we need to let it go. This is difficult to do, and we often end up desiring what we no longer have. This pattern of dissatisfaction is the essence of psychological suffering. Coming back to transversal scanning, consider how much you've already benefited from feeling and accepting sensations mostly at the surface of the body prior to starting this more advanced scanning technique. faq.question: As you know, my partner often reacts aggressively, and I’m afraid he will lose his temper and become violent when I try to be assertive with him.
faq.answer: Your concern about how your partner might react is completely valid, and it’s crucial to prioritize your safety above all else. Assertiveness cannot fix a relationship where there is ongoing abuse. If there’s a risk to your physical or emotional safety, your priority should be to seek support, ideally from a professional who can help you navigate the best course of action in a safe and supportive environment. You may also need to seriously consider whether it is in your best interest to remain in the relationship. You have the right to be and feel safe and you might need to make difficult decisions if you are not. This is also an exercise in assertiveness, but with yourself.

faq.question: My neighbor is really frustrating me! He either complains about his arthritis or about the tree leaves from my property occasionally falling on his driveway when it’s windy. I tried to help, but he still complains about the smallest things, so I’ve started to avoid interacting with him, Isn’t it bet- ter to avoid him than risk being rude to him?
faq.answer: It’s understandable that you're trying to avoid conflict, but this approach doesn’t seem to have resolved the situation. If he’s already com- plaining and reactive, it’s not the best time to assert your concerns—he’s less likely to hear you out and you may end up feeling discouraged and drained, which sounds like what has been happening. Instead, when your neighbor is expressing frustration, it’s more helpful to practice experiential ownership. Equanimity can act as a protective shield when we're faced with negativity. It helps you recognize that his reactivity is an expression of his suffering, rather than a personal attack on you. With this new perspective, you can move away from unhelpful beliefs such as, “I can never do enough to please people,” and refocus your attention in a more constructive way. Expressing your concerns assertively on a day he is calmer will be more productive. Have you been able to use the seven-step structure of mindful assertiveness? If not, let’s think about what it could sound like: 1 Once you’ve asked for a few minutes of his time to talk, thank him and state the facts. This could be something like, “For the last four months, you’ve been bringing up a lot of things you’re unhappy about.” 2 Then state what you feel, perhaps, “Every time this happens, I feel impatient and frustrated...” coxindd 27 ® os. 28.20 73427 278 STAGE 3 INTERPERSONAL STAGE 3 Now quickly say what kind of thought makes you feel this way, before your neighbor interjects with defensive statements. For example, “.,..because I assume that you want me to do something about your difficulties, and I end up feeling kind of responsible for helping you feel better.” 4 You can'then acknowledge that your thinking might be wrong: “...But of course, this might not be accurate. I often feel overly responsible for people, so I could be misreading the situation. 5 Now express what you want respectfully, such as, “I would really appre- ciate it if you could avoid directing your complaints to me, but I do care, so if you need help, I'm happy to offer what I can when I’m able.” 6 Ifyour neighbor responds positively, show your appreciation perhaps by saying, “I really appreciate that you understand—thank you.” 7 If he responds defensively or negatively, you might say, “Thanks for making time to listen,” and then go your separate ways. At this point, you’ve communicated your needs and intentions, and your neighbor now understands your boundaries—why you’re not willing to entertain his frequent complaints and how you prefer to handle issues moving, forward.
